name: Satoshi
classification_notes: Satoshi blends grotesque letterforms with geometric construction, placing it at the intersection of those two traditions. Double-storey 'a' and 'g' are default but single-storey alternates are available, reinforcing the geometric influence.
summary: A modernist sans from Indian Type Foundry that mixes grotesque letterforms with geometric influences, released free via Fontshare.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry and debuted on Fontshare in March 2021, it spans five weights from Light to Black with matching italics and a variable axis. The design draws on modernist and industrial-era typography, with double-storey 'a' and 'g' as defaults, single-storey alternates via OpenType, and a full suite of numeral styles including tabular figures and fraction components. Suited to branding, editorial and poster work where a clean but not sterile grotesque is needed.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Satoshi as a modernist sans serif that combines typically grotesque-style letterforms with geometrically-designed characters, inspired by modernism and industrial-era graphic and typographic design. The family offers ten weights from Light to Black with complementary italics, and is recommended for branding, editorial and poster design. OpenType features include ten ligatures per font, alternate forms for 'G' and 't', and multiple numeral styles.

name: General Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a rationalist sans serif with geometric capital tendencies, small apertures, and orderly letterforms recalling French mid-century typography. Sits between geometric and grotesque traditions.
summary: A rationalist sans serif from Indian Type Foundry with small apertures, geometric capitals and a clean mid-century French sensibility, available in six weights with matching italics and variable fonts.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry and released via Fontshare in March 2021, General Sans draws on rationalist and geometric sans traditions, with tightly closed apertures and orderly letterforms. Details such as a spur-free 'G', single-storey 'g', double-storey 'a', circular dots and wide 'f' and 't' give it a quiet but considered character. The family covers 135 languages across 12 static styles and two variable fonts, with case-sensitive numerals and a full range of superior and inferior figures for fraction setting.
foundry_blurb: General Sans is described by Fontshare as a rationalist sans serif whose letterforms evoke France of the 1950s — orderly yet spritely, with very small apertures that make counter-forms feel almost fully enclosed. Designed by Indian Type Foundry, it features geometric capitals, a double-storey lowercase 'a', single-storey 'g', and circular dots throughout. The family is recommended for branding, logos, posters and websites.

name: Clash Display
classification_notes: A neo-grotesque sans with tight apertures and notable stroke contrast, designed specifically for large display sizes rather than text use, placing it firmly in the display category despite its grotesque structural roots.
summary: A neo-grotesque display family from Indian Type Foundry distinguished by unusually tight apertures and pronounced stroke contrast at heavier weights.
description: Six weights from Extralight to Bold, plus a variable font, make up this display-oriented sans designed for use at large sizes. Its defining characteristic is near-closed apertures on letters like the 'c', combined with increasing stroke contrast as weights grow heavier, giving bolder cuts a noticeably pinched quality at stroke junctions. Despite its expressive details, the family is restrained enough for corporate identity work alongside editorial and banner applications.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Clash Display as a neo-grotesque sans family for large sizes, with six static styles and a variable font. Its standout feature is very small apertures — the openings at the edges of counterforms — that appear almost closed. Stroke contrast increases with weight, creating a pinched appearance at heavier cuts. The family uses proportional lining figures, a tall x-height, a double-storey lowercase 'a' and a single-storey 'g'. A companion text family, Clash Grotesk, is available for smaller sizes.

name: Ranade
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises Ranade as a sans serif in the neo-grotesk style; its defining feature is stark diagonal contrast, unusual for the genre, where thin strokes on diagonals echo classic serif construction.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans by Easha Ranade at Indian Type Foundry, distinguished by stark diagonal contrast borrowed from classic serif construction.
description: Ranade spans five weights from Thin to Bold, each with an upright and an oblique italic, and is also available as a variable font. Its tall x-height and compact spacing suit editorial and branding work, while the decision to keep 'J' and 'Q' within cap-height makes it unusually tidy at tight leading. The diagonal stroke contrast gives it more character than a typical workhorse neo-grotesque, though it remains restrained enough for corporate use.
foundry_blurb: Ranade is described by Indian Type Foundry as a neo-grotesk sans with a distinctive twist: its diagonal strokes maintain the thin-stroke contrast of classic serif typefaces, even at lighter weights. Developed by Easha Ranade at ITF's Ahmedabad studio, the family offers ten static styles across five weights with oblique italics, plus two variable fonts, and covers 135 languages across an extended Latin character set.

name: Cabinet Grotesk
classification_notes: A contemporary grotesque sans with notable stroke contrast running through all eight weights, distinguishing it from neutral grotesques and giving it a more characterful, editorial quality.
summary: A contemporary grotesque from Indian Type Foundry with visible stroke contrast that becomes most pronounced as a pinched quality at stroke connections in heavier weights.
description: Eight static weights from Thin to Black, plus a variable font, all carrying a consistent stroke-contrast model uncommon in grotesque sans designs. Double-storey 'a' and 'g' are the default with single-storey alternates available via Stylistic Sets, and the family includes 19 ligatures, case-sensitive punctuation and a Dutch IJ form. The ITF Free Font License makes it freely available through Fontshare, and it covers 135 languages across an extended Latin character set.
foundry_blurb: Cabinet Grotesk is described by Indian Type Foundry as a contemporary sans featuring a stroke contrast that sets it apart from other sans serifs. The stroke contrast becomes most visible at letterform connections in heavier weights, producing a pinched appearance. Proportional lining figures align with uppercase height, ascenders match cap height, and the family includes OpenType features such as ligatures, case-sensitive punctuation and Dutch language forms.

name: Chillax
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif with explicit Bauhaus lineage, its lowercase drawn after Herbert Bayer's 1926 Universal alphabet, placing it squarely in the geometric/constructivist tradition.
summary: A geometric sans-serif by Manushi Parikh whose lowercase letterforms derive directly from Herbert Bayer's 1926 Universal alphabet, with an added uppercase and Bauhaus-referencing OpenType alternates.
description: Designed for display and headline use, Chillax takes the constructivist geometry of the Bauhaus as its structural foundation, with lowercase forms modelled on Bayer's Universal alphabet and additional stylistic alternates that replicate the lettering from the Dessau Bauhaus building signage. Six static weights run from Extralight to Bold, supplemented by a variable font with a continuous weight axis, and OpenType features offer rounder alternate forms for a broad set of uppercase and lowercase characters. With 443 glyphs per font and support for over 135 Latin-script languages, it suits editorial, poster and brand work where a clean modernist sensibility is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Chillax as a new geometric sans aimed at larger-size display applications, bringing a 1920s modernist sensibility to contemporary editorial design. Designed by Manushi Parikh of Indian Type Foundry, its lowercase follows the pattern established by Herbert Bayer's Universal alphabet of 1926, with the uppercase being Parikh's own addition. The family is available as six static fonts or a single variable font file, includes OpenType stylistic alternates referencing Bauhaus building signage, and covers all European Latin-script languages across more than 440 glyphs per font.

name: Switzer
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Switzer as a neo-grotesque design, following the tradition of Swiss-influenced grotesques with a classic, timeless appearance.
summary: A neo-grotesque Latin-script sans from Indian Type Foundry, offered in nine weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Released on Fontshare in April 2021, Switzer follows the neo-grotesque tradition associated with Swiss type design, presenting a clean and considered alternative to the established names in that genre. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and includes variable font files covering the full weight axis. With support for 135 languages across Latin-extended scripts, it is well suited to multilingual editorial, branding and screen work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Switzer as a Latin-script typeface with 18 styles, nine of which are italics, and classify it as a neo-grotesque design. They note its classic and timeless appearance, acknowledging it may resemble earlier typefaces whilst emphasising it is a new design made for contemporary use, drawing on a tradition associated with Swiss type designers.

name: Clash Grotesk
classification_notes: Described explicitly as neo-grotesque in style, with optically monolinear strokes and a double-story 'a', though distinguished by unusually tight apertures that give it a more closed, taut character than typical grotesques.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans from Indian Type Foundry distinguished by its unusually tight apertures, available in six weights plus a variable font.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry and released on Fontshare in 2021, Clash Grotesk takes a neo-grotesque framework and tightens the apertures to the point where letters such as 'c' appear almost closed. The six static weights from Extralight to Bold are optically monolinear, with a tall x-height, large counterforms and proportional lining figures sitting level with the capitals and ascenders. A companion family, Clash Display, covers larger-size use cases, while this text-oriented family covers 135 Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Clash Grotesk as a neo-grotesque family whose defining feature is its very small apertures, giving letters an almost closed look at their openings. The design is said to be tame enough for corporate identity work yet distinctive enough for editorial use, with optically monolinear strokes across all six weights and optimisation for running-text sizes in the heavier cuts.

name: Panchang
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises it as Sans, Display. Panchang is a wide, almost panoramic sans-serif with industrial terminals and a tall x-height, designed primarily for display use at larger sizes.
summary: A wide, panoramic display sans from Indian Type Foundry, designed by Hitesh Malaviya and Barbara Bigosińska, with seven weights and a variable axis.
description: Panchang is built around unusually wide letterforms that verge on panoramic, giving it a distinctly industrial character suited to posters, editorial headlines and magazine covers. Lowercase ascenders sit taller than the capitals, and a tall x-height keeps the design open at large sizes. Most terminals end on horizontal or vertical strokes for a clean, technical look, though selective diagonal cuts on letters such as 'C', 'S' and 'g' prevent monotony, and the single-storey 'a' and 'g' add a contemporary touch.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Panchang as a wide sans-serif family whose letterforms are so broad they appear almost panoramic. The design features a tall x-height with ascenders exceeding capital height, predominantly horizontal and vertical terminals for a clean, streamlined feel, and select diagonal stroke endings on characters including 'C', 'S', 'c', 'e' and 'g' to break the monotony. Single-storey forms for 'a' and 'g', a simplified ampersand recalling the original 'Et' ligature, and a rounded lowercase 'y' round out a typeface that is broadly industrial in character but rich in considered detail.

name: Zodiak
designer: Jeremie Hornus, Gaetan Baehr, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Alisa Nowak, Theo Guillard
classification_notes: Zodiak features bracketed slab-like serifs, a vertical stress axis, and proportions reminiscent of late-nineteenth-century typefaces in the Century model, placing it at the intersection of transitional and contemporary editorial serifs.
summary: A sturdy serif family from Indian Type Foundry with bracketed slab-like serifs and Century-model proportions, available in six weights with italics and variable font support.
description: Designed by a Paris-based team under Indian Type Foundry, Zodiak draws on late-nineteenth-century typefaces in the Century tradition, with a vertical axis and near-uniform uppercase widths. The lighter weights suit extended reading whilst the extremes of Thin and Black are intended for display pairings. Decorative details appear throughout, including flag-like strokes on the numerals, a double-storey lowercase 'g' in uprights, and prominent ball terminals in the italics.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Zodiak as a font family with sturdy serifs suited to running text in its middle weights and display applications at its extremes. The letterforms reference contemporary newspaper fonts and the Century model, with bracketed slab serifs, tall x-height, and decorative numeral details. The family was designed by Jeremie Hornus, Gaetan Baehr, Jean-Baptiste Morizot, Alisa Nowak and Theo Guillard, a Paris-based team working under Indian Type Foundry.

name: Sentient
classification_notes: Classified as a serif by the foundry and designed for immersive reading; its wide proportions, large counterforms and strong capitals place it firmly in the text serif category with some display capability.
summary: A wide-proportioned text serif from Indian Type Foundry, built for long-form reading with sturdy capitals, large counterforms and a full set of OpenType alternates.
description: Designed for immersive reading, its slightly wide glyph proportions give it a visual solidity that holds up under poor printing conditions and on screen alike. The italic cuts offer distinctive touches including a closed-bowl 'g', an alternate ampersand form and 14 f-ligatures, with additional stylistic sets covering a straight-sided 'M' and a long-tailed 'Q'. Available in five weights with italics and variable font support, it covers 135 languages across the Latin script.
foundry_blurb: Sentient is a serif family designed for texts intended for immersive reading, suited to material people want to sink their teeth into. Its glyphs are slightly wide in proportion, giving the letters a visual robustness that helps them hold up even under poor printing conditions. Large counterforms aid on-screen legibility, while the italic cuts include a closed-bowl 'g', an alternate ampersand, 14 f-ligatures, and stylistic alternates for the 'M' and 'Q'. Default numerals are proportional lining figures, with oldstyle figures available via an OpenType alternate.

name: Stardom
classification_notes: Tagged as Serif, Display by Fontshare and described explicitly as a display typeface intended for extreme large sizes, placing it firmly in the display category with serif characteristics.
summary: A single-weight display serif from Indian Type Foundry, built specifically for use at extreme large sizes in headlines and poster work.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry and released through Fontshare in March 2021, this single-style serif sits squarely in the display camp, offering one Regular weight intended to perform at headline and poster scale. With 387 glyphs covering 135 languages across the Latin Extended range, it has broader coverage than its simple style count might suggest. Available under the ITF Free Font Licence, it suits branding, logos and experimental display work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Stardom as a single-weight serif display typeface, designed to be used at extreme large sizes. It is tagged for use in branding, logos, posters and experimental work, and was first made available on Fontshare in March 2021 under the ITF Free Font Licence.

name: Author
classification_notes: Classified on the page as a humanist sans serif, with visible stroke contrast in lowercase letters and diagonal stroke terminals referencing broad-pen calligraphy, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans from Indian Type Foundry designed for modern publications, with slightly expanded letterforms and diagonal stroke terminals drawn from broad-pen calligraphy.
description: Designed by Satya Rajpurohit, Author spans six weights from Extralight to Bold, each with a matching italic, plus variable font files. The uppercase letters are broadly monolinear while the lowercase carries visible stroke contrast, and the stroke terminals cut diagonally in reference to humanist calligraphic forms. Double-storey 'a' and 'g' appear in the uprights, with single-storey alternates available via OpenType, while the italics use single-storey forms throughout.
foundry_blurb: Author is a humanist sans serif family optimised for modern publications, with slightly expanded letterforms that give counterforms room to breathe for improved legibility and on-screen rendering. Six weights run from Extralight to Bold, each with an upright and italic, and the family includes both lining and oldstyle figures. Diagonal stroke terminals reference the broad-pen tradition underlying the humanist letterforms.

name: Supreme
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a constructed-style sans serif with monolinear strokes and straight-sided letterforms, placing it firmly in the geometric/engineered sans tradition rather than humanist or grotesque.
summary: A constructed geometric sans from Indian Type Foundry with monolinear strokes across seven weights and matching obliques, released free via Fontshare.
description: Designed by Jeremie Hornus and Ilya Naumoff, Supreme draws on the engineered sans tradition with straight-sided letterforms and monolinear strokes that recall technical lettering conventions. The family spans Thin to Extrabold in 14 static styles plus two variable fonts, with a moderate x-height that gives the ascenders and descenders room to breathe. Originally associated with engineering and technology branding, the style has found renewed use in screen typography and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Supreme as a large family of constructed-style sans serif fonts whose forms echo those favoured by engineers for over a century. Letters are straight-sided with monolinear strokes, and the italic styles are properly obliques rather than drawn italics. The family covers 135 languages, uses a double-storey 'a' and single-storey 'g', and is tagged for use in books, logos, magazines, posters and on-screen settings.

name: Boska
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises Boska as Serif, Display. It is a high-contrast Didone-style family with calligraphic influences, intended primarily for large-scale headline and editorial use rather than text setting.
summary: A Didone display family by Barbara Bigosinska with high stroke contrast, calligraphic inflection and slightly condensed proportions, designed for editorial headlines and signage.
description: Drawn with the high stroke contrast of nineteenth-century modern serifs and the rhythm of pointed-pen calligraphy, Boska offers six weights from Extralight to Black, each with a matching italic. The characters sit slightly condensed, especially in the uppercase, while lowercase ascenders clear the cap height; default oldstyle figures and small caps are included, with lining figures accessible via OpenType. Special glyphs such as the hashtag and @ are rendered in hairline monolinear strokes so they recede behind the alphabetic characters.
foundry_blurb: Boska is described by Indian Type Foundry as a high-contrast Didone-style family with a calligraphic flourish, intended for editorial headlines, signage and exhibition graphics. It draws from straight-axis nineteenth-century typefaces and pointed-pen calligraphy, offering six weights with italics, small caps, 13 f-ligatures, proportional oldstyle figures and hairline-stroked special characters.

name: Telma
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises it as Script; the letterforms are drawn with pen-like thick-thin contrast and informal, connected strokes, placing it squarely in the informal script category.
summary: An informal script family by Czech designer Jitka Janečková, built on thick-thin contrast with swash capitals, oldstyle figures and ligature support across five weights.
description: Designed by Jitka Janečková and released through Fontshare in 2021, Telma spans five weights from Light to Black, with thick strokes growing heavier at each step while the thins remain consistent. Several uppercase letters carry swash-like descenders, and lowercase ascenders rise above capital height, giving the face a distinctive rhythm. OpenType ligatures and contextual alternates activate automatically, making it well suited to packaging, greeting cards and magazine headlines on glossy stock.
foundry_blurb: Telma is described as a family of informal script fonts with notable thick-thin stroke contrast that lends a more formal quality to its whimsical characters. Developed by Czech designer Jitka Janečková, it features swash-style descenders on select uppercase letters, a tall x-height, proportionally-spaced oldstyle figures, and OpenType ligatures and contextual alternates. The foundry recommends it for greeting card design, packaging, glossy magazine headlines and small-business logos.

name: Bespoke Serif
designer: Jeremie Hornus, Theo Guillard, Morgane Pambrun, Alisa Nowak, Joachim Vu
classification_notes: Bespoke Serif features wedge-shaped serifs and open counterforms, positioning it as a text-optimised wedge or hybrid serif rather than a classical old-style or transitional design.
summary: A text-oriented wedge serif from Indian Type Foundry with five weights, italics and variable font support, designed as part of the broader Bespoke superfamily.
description: Designed by a team of five at Indian Type Foundry, Bespoke Serif pairs wedge-shaped serifs with large open counterforms to keep text legible at small sizes. The italics are partially cursivised rather than simply slanted, with the lowercase 'a' and 'g' switching from double-storied to single-storied forms. Proportionally-spaced oldstyle figures complete the text-focused feature set, and the family sits alongside Bespoke Sans and Bespoke Stencil as part of a coherent superfamily.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Bespoke Serif as a family designed for reading at text sizes, with wedge-shaped serifs and open counterforms to aid legibility. The italic is partially but not fully cursivised, with the 'a' and 'g' shifting to single-storied forms. Proportional oldstyle figures are included, and the family forms part of the Bespoke superfamily alongside Bespoke Sans and Bespoke Sans Stencil, suited to branding, corporate communication and editorial design.

name: Gambetta
classification_notes: Gambetta features diagonal stress, visible stroke contrast that increases with weight, and prominent wedge-shaped terminals — hallmarks of a transitional serif tradition, designed explicitly for continuous text setting.
summary: A text serif by Paul Troppmar with diagonal stress, increasing stroke contrast across five weights, and distinctive wedge-shaped terminals throughout the uppercase.
description: Designed for book and editorial settings, Gambetta offers ten styles across five weights from Light to Bold, each with an accompanying italic. The upright cuts feature wedge terminals on all uppercase vertical strokes, while open counterforms on the lowercase 'a' improve readability at text sizes. In the italics, the single-storey 'a' and double-storey 'g' with its prominent diagonal ear give the family a lively secondary texture.
foundry_blurb: Gambetta is a serif family built for text use, designed by Paul Troppmar and released on Fontshare in March 2021. The ITF describe letterforms drawn with diagonal stress and visible contrast that grows across the five weights, noting the wedge-shaped terminals on uppercase verticals, the open lowercase 'a' counterforms, and the distinctive flat-topped '3'. The italic cuts feature a single-storey 'a', a double-storey 'g' with a diagonal ear, and an open-loop 'k'. The family is intended for book and editorial design.

name: Gambarino
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Gambarino as a post-modern interpretation of the Garalde genre, with fine serifs, teardrop terminals and top-heavy capitals characteristic of that tradition, designed for display and headline use.
summary: A condensed, single-weight Garalde-derived serif by Théo Guillard, built for headlines with fine serifs, teardrop terminals and distinctively top-heavy capitals.
description: Designed by Théo Guillard and released through Fontshare in 2021, Gambarino takes the Garalde genre as its starting point but pushes it in a post-modern direction suited to display settings. Its capital letters carry oversized bowls and counterforms in their upper halves, most visible in B, E, F, P and R, while the lowercase pairs a tall x-height with unusually short descenders. The exquisitely fine serifs and teardrop terminals on characters like a, f and y give it enough personality to headline alongside a conventional Garalde body face without competing with it.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Gambarino as a condensed, single-weight serif designed for headlines, characterising it as a post-modern interpretation of the Garalde genre. The foundry notes its fine serifs paired with teardrop terminals, top-heavy capitals with larger upper bowls and counterforms, a tall x-height and short descenders, and suggests it works well alongside a conventional Garalde text face.

name: Excon
classification_notes: Excon draws on French-style sans serifs of the 1950s and 60s, referencing Roger Excoffon's top-heavy letterforms and including stroke contrast that increases through the weight range, placing it closer to humanist sans than geometric or grotesque traditions.
summary: A six-weight sans serif by Alisa Nowak that pays homage to Roger Excoffon's top-heavy letterforms, with stroke contrast that grows more pronounced from Thin to Black.
description: Designed by Alisa Nowak and released through Indian Type Foundry via Fontshare in 2021, Excon takes its cue from French sans serifs of the 1950s and 60s, particularly the top-heavy construction associated with Roger Excoffon. Stroke contrast is deliberately restrained in the lighter weights and becomes quite marked in the Black, giving the family a range of texture across its six styles. Practical details include a tall x-height, ascenders that exceed the cap height, and proportionally-spaced numerals drawn to cap height.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Excon as a versatile six-weight sans serif family that serves as a tribute to French master designer Roger Excoffon (1910–1983). The typeface's top-heavy letterforms reference Excoffon's own experimentation with this rarely-explored idea, filtered through French sans serif traditions of the 1950s and 60s. Stroke contrast increases from the lightest Thin to the heaviest Black weight, and the family's details — including a mixed double-storey 'a' with single-storey 'g', tall x-height and cap-height numerals — make it suited to corporate identity and editorial design.

name: Tanker
classification_notes: Tanker is classified by Fontshare as Sans/Display. Its uppercase-only design with rounded corners, heavy weight and condensed proportions place it firmly in the display category rather than a functional text sans.
summary: A single-weight, uppercase-only condensed display sans by Ruosi Huang, with two distinct letterform sets and universally rounded corners.
description: Designed by Ruosi Huang and released via Fontshare in August 2021, Tanker offers two versions of each capital letter: the uppercase keys deliver more experimental, dynamic forms, while the lowercase keys hold traditional sans serif caps. A secondary set of numerals is accessible via an OpenType Stylistic Set. Rounded corners soften what is otherwise a heavy, condensed presence suited to posters, banners and logotype work.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Tanker as a single-weight, uppercase-only condensed sans serif with two different versions of each letter available through the uppercase and lowercase keys respectively. The lowercase caps offer more traditional sans serif forms while the uppercase caps are experimental and dynamic. All corners are rounded, lending a soft quality despite the heavy weight, and a second set of alternate numerals is available via an OpenType Stylistic Set.

name: Quilon
classification_notes: Described as a high-contrast sans serif drawing on 19th century English grotesques and 1990s postmodernism; the dramatic stroke contrast across weights pushes it toward display grotesque territory.
summary: A high-contrast display sans from Indian Type Foundry, blending 19th century English grotesque structure with postmodern stroke drama across four weights.
description: Stroke contrast increases markedly as the weights progress from Regular to Bold, making the heavier cuts particularly suited to poster and headline work. Its letterforms reference both Victorian-era grotesques and 1990s postmodernism, giving the family an unusual dual lineage. Named after Quilon, the historical name for Kollam in Kerala, India, the family supports 135 languages across Latin Extended script coverage and ships with a variable font axis.
foundry_blurb: Quilon is a high-contrast sans serif family whose design features span centuries, combining elements of 19th century English grotesques with 1990s postmodernism. The stroke contrast increases dramatically as weights progress, making it well suited to display typography. Each font includes 391 glyphs. The name Quilon refers to the historical name for Kollam, a city in Kerala, India.

name: Neco
classification_notes: Neco features prominent wedge-shaped serifs, a diagonal axis of stress and high stroke contrast, placing it in the wedge or Latin serif tradition rather than classical old-style or transitional categories.
summary: A serif family by Jitka Janečková with wedge-shaped serifs, high stroke contrast and true cursive italics, suited to editorial and headline work.
description: Designed by Jitka Janečková and released on Fontshare in 2021, Neco spans four weights from Regular to Black, each with a matching italic drawn as true cursive forms rather than sloped uprights. The wedge-shaped serifs and diagonal stress axis give it a distinctive tension that intensifies markedly at heavier weights, making the Black particularly forceful. Proportional oldstyle figures are the default, with tabular, lining and small-cap numerals also included, alongside f-ligatures and full fraction support.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Neco as a serif family intended for both headlines and shorter-to-medium body text. They highlight its chunky wedge serifs, diagonal stress axis and high stroke contrast, noting that contrast increases from weight to weight. The Black and Black Italic are called out as particularly dynamic. Lowercase letters have a tall x-height with ascenders that clear the cap height, and the italics are true cursive forms. Recommended for editorial design, invitations and event graphics.

name: Alpino
classification_notes: Classified by the foundry as a humanist sans, with double-storey 'a' and 'g', proportional oldstyle figures, and rounded stroke terminals that become more pronounced in heavier weights — all hallmarks of humanist sans design with editorial intent.
summary: A humanist sans from Indian Type Foundry with a small x-height, oldstyle figures and distinctively rounded stroke terminals, aimed at magazine and editorial work.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry and released via Fontshare in March 2021, Alpino is a humanist sans built around a small x-height with wide capitals that sit comfortably alongside generous ascenders and descenders. Proportional oldstyle figures and subtly rounded stroke corners give it warmth, while distinctive details — a bearded capital G without a crossbar, a double-storey g with a flamboyant ear, and a curved y tail — lend it some personality without being loud. Available in six static weights from Thin to Black plus a variable font, with coverage across 135 languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Alpino as a humanist sans with a small x-height optimised for magazine design and editorial applications. The strokes feature slightly rounded corners that become more pronounced in heavier weights, and the family includes proportional oldstyle figures. Distinctive letterforms include a capital G with a beard but no crossbar, double-storey a and g, a g with a flamboyant ear, a y tail ending in a curve, and a detached cedilla form.

name: Erode
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises Erode as Serif with display characteristics; the typeface features clear stroke contrast, long ascenders and open counters, positioning it firmly as a high-contrast display serif suited to headlines and pull quotes rather than continuous text.
summary: A high-contrast Latin serif from the Indian Type Foundry, designed for display use with long ascenders and open counters across five weights.
description: Named after a city in Tamil Nadu and developed in 2015 by Nikhil Ranganathan and ITF, Erode offers clear stroke contrast and generous ascenders that make it well suited to pull quotes, headlines and poster work. The family spans five weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching italic, plus variable font axes for weight. With 135 languages supported across extended Latin script, it covers broad editorial use cases for display-size settings.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Erode as a serif typeface for the Latin script, named after a city in Tamil Nadu, featuring five weights with clear stroke contrast, long ascenders and open counters. They position it as an excellent choice for editorial designers seeking a typeface for eye-catching pull quotes, headlines and display text, noting it was developed in 2015 by Nikhil Ranganathan and ITF.

name: Pally
classification_notes: Pally is classified as a sans-serif with a highly informal, wobbly character. Its monolinear strokes, asymmetric letterforms and atypical stroke angles place it firmly outside neutral grotesque or geometric territory.
summary: A deliberately wobbly, informal sans-serif by Jean-Baptiste Morizot, with asymmetric letterforms and atypical stroke angles that give it a playful, characterful rhythm.
description: Designed by Jean-Baptiste Morizot and released through Fontshare in 2021, Pally pairs monolinear strokes with intentionally uneven letterforms: the left and right sides of many glyphs differ in size and height, strokes meet at unexpected angles, and the ascenders of lowercase letters clear the cap height. The character set includes a double-storey 'a', a single-storey 'g' and a Jensonian 'e', and the family is available as three static weights plus a variable font covering 135 languages. It suits headlines, signage and editorial contexts aimed at children's services or informal branding where typographic eccentricity is an asset.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Pally as a highly informal sans-serif designed by Jean-Baptiste Morizot, whose wobbly letterforms, atypical stroke angles and mismatched left and right sides give it far more character than most sans-serifs. They suggest it as an excellent choice for headlines or signage in children's services contexts, and note that once you understand the different rhythm it offers, its underlying typographic sophistication becomes apparent.

name: Bespoke Sans
classification_notes: Classified by Fontshare as Sans; the page describes it explicitly as a humanist sans serif with open counterforms, diagonal stroke-endings referencing broad-pen calligraphy, and double-storied 'a' and 'g' — all hallmarks of the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans from Indian Type Foundry with open counterforms, diagonal stroke-endings and oldstyle figures across five weights with matching italics.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry, Bespoke Sans draws on broad-pen calligraphic tradition through its diagonal sheared ascender terminals, which run consistently through the design. The upright styles use double-storied 'a' and 'g', switching to single-storied forms in the italics, and proportionally-spaced oldstyle figures sit comfortably alongside lowercase text. Ten static styles span Light to Extrabold with italics throughout, plus variable font support, making it adaptable for branding, editorial, corporate communication and wayfinding.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Bespoke Sans as a humanist sans serif family featuring large, open counterforms for strong legibility. The diagonal stroke-endings on lowercase ascenders reference the broad-pen calligraphy that underlies the letterform design, while double-storied 'a' and 'g' in the uprights become single-storied in the italics. Oldstyle proportional figures harmonise with the lowercase, and the family is recommended for branding, corporate communication, editorial design, signage and wayfinding.

name: Nippo
classification_notes: Classified as Display by Fontshare; letterforms are derived from sports jersey lettering with monolinear strokes, straightened curves and diagonal joins that push it firmly into display territory rather than conventional geometric sans.
summary: A monolinear display sans drawn from sports jersey lettering, with straightened curves and diagonal joins across five weights plus a variable font.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry, Nippo takes its cues from athletics typography, stripping letterforms of most curves in favour of horizontal, vertical and diagonal strokes. Selective stroke thinning on crossbars prevents letters from massing too darkly, while six stylistic sets offer alternates for characters including G, L, M, N, R, T and the numeral 4. The family covers 135 languages and includes a weight-axis variable font.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Nippo as a sans with an instantly recognisable source of inspiration from athletics, designed specifically for sports jerseys. Curves are straightened into horizontal and vertical lines held together by diagonal elements, with selective thinning on crossbars to prevent letterforms getting too dark. Several stylistic sets provide alternates for characters such as G, L, M, N, R, T and a closed-topped 4.

name: Pencerio
classification_notes: Pencerio is a monolinear formal script based on Spencerian penmanship, with hairline strokes and no contrast — placing it firmly in the script category as a formal, calligraphic script.
summary: A monolinear formal script by Manushi Parikh, adapting Spencerian penmanship into a single hairline weight with ornate capitals and a large x-height.
description: Rather than reproducing the characteristic thick-thin contrast of traditional Spencerian scripts, this face strips the letterforms back to thin, monolinear strokes throughout — preserving the slope and ornamental loops of the historical model without the pressure-driven shading. Capital letters carry multiple loops, swells and swirls, with several featuring long descending swash elements that shape strong word silhouettes. A character set of 675 glyphs includes a substantial bank of alternates and ligatures accessible via standard OpenType features, making it suited to calling cards, invitations and certificates.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Pencerio as a new take on formal Spencerian handwriting, reinterpreted through thin, monolinear strokes rather than the pressure-contrast of a pointed pen. Designed by Manushi Parikh, it features ornate uppercase letters with loops and swash elements, a large x-height in the lowercase, and around 300 alternate glyphs on top of the standard character set to enable on-the-fly ligature creation. They position it for formal occasions requiring a contemporary typographic approach, including weddings, certificates and invitations.

name: Sharpie
classification_notes: Though categorised as Display by Fontshare, Sharpie draws directly from sign-painting and marker lettering traditions, with angular, slanted letterforms and strong stroke contrast that place it firmly in informal script-influenced display territory.
summary: A sign-painterly display family from Indian Type Foundry, drawn as if written quickly with a flat brush and named after the marker that inspired its angular letterforms.
description: Five weights from Light to Black, all slanted and non-connecting, with stroke contrast that builds from modest in Light to dramatic in Black. Single-storey 'a' and 'g', a short x-height, and cap height aligned with ascenders give it a consistent lettering-script rhythm across the range. A variable font is included alongside the five static styles, and the family covers 135 languages across Latin Extended scripts.
foundry_blurb: Sharpie is described by ITF as an informal script family named after a type of marker, with letterforms that evoke quick sign-painting with a flat brush. All letters are slanted and non-connecting, with angular strokes and stroke contrast that grows markedly from Light to Black. Designed by Théo Guillard and debuted on Fontshare in March 2021.

name: Plein
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist-style sans serif with open, legible forms and virtually monolinear strokes, placing it squarely in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A humanist sans serif by Berlin-based designer Inga Plönnigs, offering five weights with italics and variable font support across 135 languages.
description: Drawn with virtually monolinear strokes and open letterforms, Plein is suited to editorial work, branding and signage where legibility is a priority. The upright cuts use double-storey 'a' and 'g', switching to single-storey in the italics, while the default numerals are proportional oldstyle figures with lining figures available via OpenType. Inga Plönnigs, a graduate of the Type & Media programme at KABK in The Hague, designed the family and it debuted on Fontshare in March 2021.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Plein as a humanist-style sans serif family with clear, open and legible forms drawn with monolinear strokes. Available in five weights from Light to Black, each with an italic, the family also includes variable fonts. Designed by Inga Plönnigs, a Berlin-based independent type designer and KABK graduate, it is positioned for branding, corporate design, editorial work, signage and navigation projects.

name: Bespoke Stencil
designer: Jeremie Hornus, Theo Guillard, Morgane Pambrun, Alisa Nowak, Joachim Vu
classification_notes: Classified by Fontshare as Sans/Display, Bespoke Stencil is a humanist sans serif with stencil-style breaks at stroke junctions, sharing its underlying structure with the Bespoke Sans family.
summary: A humanist sans serif with stencil-style stroke breaks, designed by Indian Type Foundry as part of the broader Bespoke superfamily.
description: Each letterform interrupts at the point where a broad-pen writer might pause, giving the typeface its stencil character without sacrificing legibility. Large, open counterforms support that legibility, while oldstyle proportional figures harmonise with the lowercase. Upright weights use double-storied 'a' and 'g'; the italics switch to single-storied forms. The family runs from Light to Extrabold with matching italics and includes variable font versions.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Bespoke Stencil as a humanist sans serif family whose letterforms break at the points where strokes would pause if written with a broad pen. The design shares its underlying structure with Bespoke Sans, features large open counterforms for legibility, and offers oldstyle proportional figures that work especially well with the lowercase. Designed collaboratively by five designers, the family forms part of what Fontshare call 'the Bespoke superfamily' alongside Bespoke Sans and Bespoke Serif.

name: Pramukh Rounded
classification_notes: A condensed geometric sans-serif with rounded terminals on all strokes, giving it a friendly quality despite its narrow proportions. The modernist typographic vocabulary and oblique italics align it with geometric sans conventions.
summary: A very condensed geometric sans with rounded stroke terminals across eight weights and matching oblique italics, designed by Aarya Purohit and the Indian Type Foundry.
description: All strokes terminate in semicircular curves, softening what would otherwise be a strictly formal condensed sans. The family spans eight weights from Extralight to Black, each with an oblique italic companion, and is available as a variable font. Suited to corporate identity, editorial layouts and poster work where a narrow sans is required but a degree of warmth is wanted alongside the formal structure.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Pramukh Rounded as a very condensed sans serif in which all strokes end in round semicircular curves. Its 16 styles cover eight weights each with a companion oblique italic, and the design is positioned as the warmer, more open counterpart to the non-rounded Pramukh family. ITF recommend it for corporate identity, editorial and poster work wherever a narrow sans needs a friendlier character.

name: Amulya
classification_notes: Classified as humanist sans by the foundry; letterforms show warmth and personality through curved out-strokes and open counterforms typical of humanist design principles.
summary: A humanist sans by Joana Correia with curved out-strokes and open counterforms that give it warmth across text and display settings.
description: Designed by Joana Correia and released via Fontshare in 2021, Amulya distinguishes itself among humanist sans-serifs through curved out-strokes on several letters, an unusual choice that adds character without sacrificing legibility. Open counterforms keep reading comfortable across screen and print, and the family spans Light to Bold with matching italics plus variable font support. Suited to corporate identity, signage, magazines and long-form text. The name means 'precious' in Hindi.
foundry_blurb: Joana Correia's Amulya is described by Indian Type Foundry as a friendly, outstanding humanistic sans whose letterforms are both playful and appealing. Curved out-strokes lend personality while very open counterforms ensure high legibility. The foundry recommends it for corporate identity, signage, and text use both onscreen and in print, noting that 'Amulya' translates as 'precious' in Hindi.

name: Synonym
classification_notes: Described explicitly as humanist sans serif, with stroke contrast, diagonal terminals and double-storey letterforms that distinguish it from monolinear grotesques.
summary: A six-weight humanist sans by Hugo Dumont, with visible stroke contrast, diagonal terminals and double-storey 'a' and 'g' across all weights.
description: Designed by Paris-based Hugo Dumont and released via Fontshare in 2021, Synonym sits apart from purely monolinear humanist sans serifs through its moderate stroke contrast and the diagonal cuts on the arms of letters such as 'E', 'F' and 'T'. The family runs from Extralight to Bold with a variable font included, and covers 135 languages across an extended Latin range. It suits editorial and branding work where legibility and a degree of personality are both required.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Synonym as a family of six humanist sans serif fonts suited to branding and editorial design, noting its non-monolinear stroke contrast, double-storied 'a' and 'g', diagonal stroke terminals, moderately positioned x-height, and proportionally-spaced lining numerals.

name: Bevellier
classification_notes: Bevellier is a condensed, spurless, monolinear sans-serif with rounded corners and formal reduction, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition despite its display orientation.
summary: A condensed, spurless sans-serif from Indian Type Foundry with rounded corners and a monolinear stroke across eight weights and matching italics.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry and released on Fontshare in 2021, Bevellier is defined by its narrow proportions, rounded-off corners and the removal of spurs on letters such as 'm', 'n' and 'r'. The crossbar of the lowercase 't' falls short of the full vertical stroke width, reinforcing the typeface's formal reduction. Italics mirror the spurless approach but replace the angular junctions of the upright with curved forms, giving the two styles a distinct internal logic.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Bevellier as a family of condensed and simplified sans-serif fonts whose most noticeable traits are narrow letterforms and rounded-off corners. Formal reduction is central to the design, visible in the abbreviated crossbar of the lowercase 't' and the elimination of spurs on arched letters. The italics share the spurless construction but feature more symmetrical, curved junctions where the uprights are angular. The family spans eight weights from Extralight to Black, each with an upright and italic, plus variable fonts, and supports 135 languages.

name: Rowan
classification_notes: A text-optimised serif with chunky serifs and stroke endings designed for long-form reading at both normal and small sizes, placing it firmly in the text serif tradition with humanist influences.
summary: A text serif by Inga Plönnigs, optimised for long-form reading with chunky serifs and stroke endings that hold up at small sizes.
description: Designed by Inga Plönnigs, a Type & Media graduate from KABK in The Hague, Rowan covers five weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching italic, and is also available as a variable font. Its distinctive chunky serifs and stroke endings maintain legibility at small sizes, while oldstyle figures are set as the default with lining figures available via OpenType. A stylistic set offers ascending lowercase letters without top-serifs, compensated by swelling flairs at the stem tops to preserve typographic colour.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Rowan as a serif family intended for setting long passages of text for immersive reading, usable at both normal and very small sizes due to its chunky serifs and stroke endings. The family includes five weights with uprights and italics, oldstyle figures by default, lining figures via OpenType, and a stylistic set for ascenders without top-serifs. It was developed by Berlin-based type designer Inga Plönnigs, a graduate of the Type & Media programme at KABK in The Hague.

name: Comico
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises Comico as Handwritten. Its all-caps letterforms mimic comic-book lettering with a deliberate pixelated quality at large sizes, placing it firmly in the display/handwritten camp rather than a conventional script.
summary: An all-caps comic-book lettering font by Norwegian designer Frode Helland, with nearly 1,000 contextual alternates to mimic natural handwriting variation.
description: Designed by Frode Helland, Comico draws on the lettering traditions of comic books, with all-caps forms that carry a slight left lean and counterforms drawn with straight lines. Its principal feature is a large set of ligatures and alternate glyphs, activated via the OpenType contextual alternates feature, which introduce the variation needed to avoid the mechanical repetition typical of single-weight handwriting fonts. At large sizes the letterforms read as deliberately pixelated, making it more a display novelty than a workhorse text face.
foundry_blurb: Comico is described by ITF as an informal handwriting font whose all-caps letterforms appear as though written with a slight left lean, evoking the lettering style of comic books. Its character set includes close to 1,000 special glyphs — ligatures and alternate letterforms — that activate automatically via the OpenType contextual alternates feature to increase the impression of genuine handwriting. The font is the work of Norwegian type designer Frode Helland.

name: Bonny
classification_notes: A highly condensed display serif with strong stroke contrast, flourished terminals and exaggerated proportions typical of decorative display typefaces rather than text serifs.
summary: A condensed display serif by Barbara Bigosinska with high stroke contrast, flourished terminals and deliberately caricatured uppercase proportions.
description: Designed by Barbara Bigosinska and released through Indian Type Foundry, Bonny is built for large-scale display use where its details can breathe. The letterforms are strikingly condensed with marked stroke contrast, and thin strokes frequently end in calligraphic flourishes that echo the short, delicate serifs. Uppercase letters such as 'B', 'E' and 'R' are intentionally top-heavy, lending the face an exuberant quality suited to greeting cards, packaging and poster work. Oldstyle numerals, 13 f-ligatures and a contextual alternate 'j' round out a glyph set covering 135 languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Bonny as a family of decorative serif fonts designed for display typography, with very condensed letterforms and high stroke contrast. Thin strokes terminate in flourishes that complement the typeface's short, delicate serifs, and uppercase letters are notably top-heavy, giving characters an almost caricature-like quality. Each style includes oldstyle figures, 13 f-ligatures and a contextual alternate 'j'. Indian Type Foundry recommend setting Bonny at large point sizes for use on greeting cards and in packaging design.

name: Technor
classification_notes: Constructed sans with monolinear strokes, flat sides, and single-storey letterforms — characteristics of geometric sans design, built explicitly for display use.
summary: A constructed geometric sans by Jean-Baptiste Morizot, built for display use across seven weights with monolinear strokes, flat sides and rounded terminals.
description: Designed for Indian Type Foundry and released via Fontshare in 2021, Technor is a wide, display-oriented geometric sans with monolinear strokes and flat letter sides. Characters feature rounded tops and bottoms where tradition allows, while letters such as 'E', 'F' and 'T' retain their flat terminals; single-storey 'a' and 'g' keep the forms simple. Inter-character spacing is deliberately tight, and the tall x-height of the lowercase makes it particularly suited to large-scale poster and headline work.
foundry_blurb: Technor is described as a seven-weight family of constructed sans serif fonts with flat sides and monolinear strokes. Characters have rounded tops and bottoms where tradition allows, and spacing is intentionally very tight. Jean-Baptiste Morizot designed it for display applications, with the intention that the larger the size, the more effectively its unique design language communicates.

name: Aktura
classification_notes: Aktura combines traditional textura-style gothic lowercase forms with ornate lombardic capitals, a historically attested pairing. The foundry categorises it as Serif, Blackletter, Display.
summary: A single-weight blackletter by Gaetan Baehr pairing textura-style gothic lowercase letters with ornate lombardic capitals, reviving a historical style-mixing convention.
description: The lowercase follows the close-packed vertical strokes of textura gothics familiar from newspaper nameplates and diploma lettering, while the uppercase draws on the decorative swash forms of lombardic initials. The foundry notes the pairing echoes a pre-printing-press convention, where lombardic caps served as uppercase counterparts to gothic minuscules. Best suited to display settings, posters and logotypes rather than running text, and the elaborate caps work particularly well as drop capitals alongside serif body typefaces.
foundry_blurb: Aktura is described by Indian Type Foundry as a medieval-style blackletter with lowercase letters that take a digital-calligraphic approach to traditional textura forms, and ornate lombardic capitals that draw on a historical practice of mixing the two styles. The caps are intentionally elaborate and are recommended for use as initials or drop caps rather than all-caps setting, though wide tracking may make short headlines viable. The font is a single weight designed by Gaetan Baehr and debuted on Fontshare in July 2021 under the ITF Free Font License.

name: Bespoke Slab
designer: Jérémie Hornus, Théo Guillard, Morgane Pambrun, Alisa Nowak, Joachim Vu
classification_notes: Classified as a slab serif by the foundry itself; humanist in character with broad-pen references, open counterforms and diagonal stroke terminals typical of humanist slab construction.
summary: A humanist slab serif from Indian Type Foundry with five weights, matching italics and a variable axis, built around open counterforms and diagonal stroke endings drawn from broad-pen calligraphy.
description: Designed by a five-person team at ITF and released via Fontshare in 2021, Bespoke Slab uses large open counterforms to keep text legible across sizes, from body copy to poster work. Ascender tops cut at diagonal shears carry a subtle calligraphic reference through every weight, and the italic cuts swap double-storey 'a' and 'g' for single-storey forms. Proportionally-spaced oldstyle figures sit comfortably alongside the lowercase, making the family well suited to editorial, branding and wayfinding contexts.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Bespoke Slab as a humanist slab serif family with large open counterforms for maximum legibility in text. It offers five weights from Light to Bold, each with an upright and italic, plus variable fonts. Diagonal shear stroke-endings reference the broad pen, while the italic cuts use single-storey 'a' and 'g'. Oldstyle proportional figures harmonise with the lowercase. The foundry position it for branding, corporate communication, editorial design, signage and wayfinding.

name: Array
classification_notes: Array mimics electronic dot-matrix displays, with letterforms built entirely from dots on a grid. Fontshare categorises it as Display, and its construction is too stylised and novelty-driven to sit in any standard sans-serif subcategory.
summary: A six-style dot-matrix display family from Indian Type Foundry, with letterforms constructed entirely from dots on a regular grid.
description: Each letterform in Array is built on a strict dot grid, with capitals sitting 10 dots tall and lowercase letters scaled proportionally. Weight variation is achieved by widening the stems rather than changing dot size, running from two dots wide in Regular to four in Bold. A Wide variant adds lateral spacing to each weight, making six styles in total, suited to posters, signage and experimental editorial work at large sizes.
foundry_blurb: Array is described by Indian Type Foundry as a small family of electronic display style fonts, with all letterforms made from dots on a grid. Developed for Fontstore by Norwegian designer Frode Helland, it offers three weights and two widths, and is recommended for experimental graphic design, electronic displays, and editorial projects where the fonts will be used at large sizes.

name: Britney
classification_notes: Fontshare classifies it as Script, but ITF describe it as living on the border between sans serif, cursive and script. Its very high contrast, double stem strokes and clean pointed-brush shapes place it firmly in the display category rather than a functional script.
summary: A high-contrast display family from Indian Type Foundry that borrows pointed-brush forms without the roughness, keeping stems clean and doubled for a distinctive graphic effect.
description: Four weights from Light to Ultra, plus a variable axis, give the family a wide tonal range while maintaining its characteristic double-stroke stems and sharp contrast. Capital letters are deliberately restrained, avoiding loops and flourishes so all-caps settings remain legible. Suited to packaging, album covers, magazine headlines and anywhere a small number of characters needs to carry strong visual presence.
foundry_blurb: Britney is described by ITF as a quirky display family with very high contrast and double strokes on the stems, sitting on the border between sans serif, cursive and script. The shapes draw loosely on pointed-brush experiments but are kept clean and crisp rather than handwritten, with simple capitals that allow all-caps use. It is intended for packaging, headlines, album covers and magazines.

name: Styro
classification_notes: A modernist stencil display face with heavily condensed, near-monospaced letterforms and a reductionist geometric vocabulary drawing on Bauhaus precedents. Classified as display due to its strong optical character and headline-oriented application.
summary: A modernist stencil family by Aarya Purohit at Indian Type Foundry, drawing on Bauhaus letterform principles across eight weights with a reductionist geometric vocabulary.
description: Designed at Indian Type Foundry, Styro uses thin interior lines to express counterforms and applies simple geometric terminals throughout, recalling Josef Albers' stencil work at the Bauhaus whilst retaining separate upper and lowercase characters. All eight weights are virtually monospaced, with outer stroke thickness increasing progressively from Extralight to Black. The family suits editorial work centred on modern art and design, as well as logos and posters where a stark, condensed presence is needed.
foundry_blurb: Styro is described by ITF as a family of modernist-style stencil fonts in eight weights from Thin to Black. The characters are very condensed and virtually monospaced, with counterforms expressed as thin internal lines running vertically through the letters. Diacritical marks and punctuation follow the same thin-line treatment. ITF note a resemblance to Josef Albers' Bauhaus stencil letters, and position Styro as an excellent choice for editorial design about modern art and design.

name: Recia
classification_notes: Classified as serif on Fontshare; features wedge-shaped serifs, high x-height, slightly condensed proportions and low stroke contrast, placing it in the contemporary or transitional serif camp rather than a classical oldstyle.
summary: A contemporary serif from Indian Type Foundry with wedge-shaped serifs, a high x-height and true italics, designed for demanding text settings.
description: Designed by Carlos De Toro as his debut with ITF, Recia is a contemporary serif built for continuous reading in conditions where sturdy letterforms are needed, such as low-quality print. Stroke contrast is deliberately restrained in lighter weights but becomes more legible in Bold, while the thicker strokes remain chunky throughout. The five italic styles are true italics with a cursive structure, and the family includes proportional oldstyle figures as the default numeral style via OpenType.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Recia as Carlos De Toro's debut typeface with ITF, a contemporary-style serif family in 10 styles with true-italic designs featuring cursive letter structure. The high x-height and slightly condensed characters carry low stroke contrast overall, with wedge-shaped serifs throughout, and the family is optimised for running text in less-than-optimal printing environments.

name: Rosaline
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises it as Script; it is a connected, brush-lettered casual script with high stroke contrast and a bouncy baseline, placing it firmly in the informal script tradition.
summary: A connected brush-script by Indian Type Foundry with wide letterforms, high stroke contrast and a lively bouncing baseline suited to identity and stationery work.
description: Inspired by brush-lettering, Rosaline uses connected letterforms that link continuously across a word, including the '@' sign, making e-mail address settings look hand-written. Its oldstyle numerals set it apart from most informal scripts, and the rhythmic bounce in the baseline gives text an air of having been written quickly but confidently. A single Regular style covers 135 languages across an extended Latin range.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Rosaline as a casual script face inspired by brush-lettering, with wide letterforms and high stroke contrast. As a fully connected script, every letter joins the next, and even the '@' connects for convincing e-mail address settings. Text set in Rosaline reads as if written quickly, with a slight bounce throughout, and oldstyle figures distinguish it from other informal scripts. ITF suggest it suits shop, café and restaurant identities, as well as holiday cards and invitations.

name: Melodrama
classification_notes: Categorised as Sans/Display on Fontshare; its defining characteristic is extreme stroke contrast paired with geometric letterforms, placing it firmly in display territory rather than conventional sans-serif usage.
summary: A high-contrast geometric sans from Indian Type Foundry with over three dozen decorative uppercase ligatures, designed for headlines and masthead work.
description: Stroke contrast increases with each weight as the thin strokes remain fixed while the thick verticals grow heavier, creating an increasingly dramatic ratio from Light through to Bold. The family includes more than three dozen decorative uppercase ligatures, covering pairs such as CH and overlapping O combinations, with lowercase ligatures also supported. Its geometric construction, double-storey 'a' and 'g', and oldstyle figure alternates round out a display typeface well suited to fashion editorial, branding and poster work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Melodrama as a family of five sans serif fonts with very high stroke contrast, where vertical strokes are roughly twice as thick as horizontals even at Light weight, and contrast intensifies at heavier weights. Its standout feature is a large set of decorative ligatures including uppercase pairs and L-ligatures. The geometric structure and glamorous character make it an excellent choice for headlines, mastheads and style websites.

name: Hoover
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises Hoover as Slab; its letterforms are built entirely from straight strokes with no curves, making it a geometric, monolinear slab serif with a distinctly collegiate character.
summary: A monolinear slab serif by Gaetan Baehr built entirely from straight strokes, with no curves anywhere in its letterforms.
description: Every character in Hoover is constructed from horizontal, vertical and diagonal lines only, eliminating all curved or rounded forms from the alphabet. The result immediately reads as collegiate or athletic, recalling over a century of Anglo-American sports graphics. Five static weights from Thin to Bold are available alongside a variable font, with coverage for 135 Latin-script languages.
foundry_blurb: Hoover is described by ITF as a family of five monolinear slab serif fonts whose most striking feature is the complete absence of curved or rounded parts. All letterforms are built from straight strokes, evoking Anglo-American collegiate graphics and athletic branding traditions. The foundry suggests it works equally well for educational institutions, sports teams and editorial contexts.

name: Chubbo
classification_notes: The foundry classifies Chubbo as Display; it is a slab serif with rounded terminals throughout, giving it a soft, expressive character suited to large-size settings.
summary: A rounded slab serif from Indian Type Foundry with five weights and genuinely redrawn cursive italics, available as static and variable fonts.
description: Chubbo's slab serifs end in rounded terminals across all five weights, lending the family a soft and approachable quality at poster and headline sizes. Its italics are a distinct contribution from type designer Rafał Buchner: rather than sloped versions of the upright forms, they are redrawn in a more cursive style that retains the feel of a pen stroke. OpenType alternates provide single-storey forms for the 'g' and 'y', and open-loop descender variants for the italic cuts.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Chubbo as a family of expressive slab serif fonts suited to large-sized text in posters, brochures, website headlines and apps. They note that its rounded terminals give it a soft, friendly quality, and highlight the italic styles as the most exciting part of the design, redrawn in a cursive style rather than simply sloped. The family ships as ten static fonts and two variable fonts, one for uprights and one for italics.

name: Trench Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif with very prominent slabs and large ink-traps, designed by Indian Type Foundry as part of the Trench superfamily. The exaggerated serifs and geometric construction place it firmly in the slab category.
summary: A slab serif from Indian Type Foundry with exaggerated serifs and prominent ink-traps, intended for display and large-size setting rather than continuous body text.
description: Part of the broader Trench superfamily alongside Trench Round and Trench Sans, this slab is built around very large serifs and conspicuous ink-traps that give it a distinctive, almost mechanical character. It suits headlines, posters, logos and magazine layouts where the weight and structure of the slab can read at scale. Stylistic sets offer alternate forms including a single-storied capital I without serifs, a double-storied g and variants of M, N, W and w. Available in five static weights plus a variable font, with coverage across 135 languages.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Trench Slab as part of the Trench superfamily, which also includes Trench Round and Trench Sans. The slab serifs are described as very, very large and equally prominent to the design's ink-traps. It is intended for larger-than-normal reading sizes, shorter text passages or large display applications, and includes stylistic alternates for several uppercase and lowercase characters.

name: RX100
classification_notes: RX100 is explicitly described as a monospaced sans serif; its fixed-width letterforms, condensed proportions and ink traps are characteristic of a monospaced typeface designed for code and typewritten aesthetics.
summary: A condensed monospaced sans serif from Indian Type Foundry with tall ascenders, large ink traps and 22 OpenType alternates, suited to code rendering and typewritten aesthetics.
description: Released on Fontshare in March 2021, RX100 takes the fixed-width format and pushes it towards an unusually narrow proportion, giving capital letters a particularly compressed appearance in mixed-case settings. Tall ascenders clear the capitals decisively, and oversized diacritical marks aid character recognition at small sizes, making it practical for programming environments. Large ink traps maintain even colour in small text, while 22 alternate glyphs and 10 arrow characters offer compositional flexibility.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe RX100 as a monospaced sans serif with notably condensed letterforms, even by fixed-width standards. They highlight its tall ascenders, large diacritical marks designed for easy recognition, and ink traps that keep small text colour even. The face evokes electrified typewriters of the 1970s and 1980s, and ships with 22 alternate uppercase, lowercase and numeral glyphs accessible via OpenType features, plus 10 arrow characters.

name: Kola
classification_notes: Categorised as Display by the foundry; a stencil-style sans serif with rounded stroke endings and an LED-display quality, making it unsuitable for running text and clearly display-oriented.
summary: A stencil sans serif by Paris-based designer Jean-Baptiste Morizot, with rounded stroke endings and proportions that recall LED display lettering.
description: Designed by Jean-Baptiste Morizot and released through Fontshare in 2021, Kola applies a stencil treatment to a condensed sans structure, with rounded terminals and large counterforms that remain legible at scale. The x-height is tall, with ascenders reaching cap height, and the diacritics extend conspicuously above both — a considered choice for multilingual display use across 135 languages. Stencil bridges are placed dynamically on letterforms such as the 'K', 'j' and 'r', giving the face a degree of personality within an otherwise restrained, near-geometric skeleton.
foundry_blurb: Kola is described by the foundry as a technoid stencil-style sans serif, designed by Paris-based Jean-Baptiste Morizot. All stroke endings are rounded, producing an effect reminiscent of LED display lettering. Its tall x-height, oversized diacritics and large counterforms keep it legible at small sizes, though it is considered best suited to large-scale use in logo design and concert posters. The stencil bridges are notably novel in characters such as the 'K', 'j' and 'r'.

name: Boxing
classification_notes: Boxing uses heavy horizontal strokes that exceed the vertical strokes, a defining trait of reverse-contrast type. Its extended, wide letterforms and extreme serif weight place it squarely in the western or latin display tradition.
summary: A single-weight extended display face with reverse contrast, where horizontal strokes and serifs dramatically outweigh the verticals in the tradition of 19th-century western and European poster type.
description: Designed by Ruosi Huang and released via Fontshare in October 2021, Boxing draws on the reverse-contrast letterforms found on Wild West-era American posters and mid-19th-century European printing, as well as the psychedelic graphics of the 1960s and 70s. All letters are wide and extended, with the horizontal elements and serifs carrying an exaggerated weight that inverts conventional stroke logic. The single Regular style suits poster headlines, flyers, logo work and editorial display settings where a bold historical reference is needed.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Boxing as an extended western font with reverse contrast, meaning its horizontal elements are heavier than its verticals or diagonals, most notably in its extreme serifs. They note that while the style evokes the Wild West, it also appeared on 19th-century European posters and later in 1960s and 70s psychedelic graphics, making its range of application broader than the name alone might suggest. Suggested uses include posters, flyers, logo design and newspaper headlines.

name: Zina
classification_notes: Zina is categorised as Display by Fontshare; it is a Didone-style inline serif whose defining feature is decorative strokes running through thick letterform bodies, making it unsuitable for body text and firmly display-category.
summary: A single-weight inline Didone serif from Indian Type Foundry, designed for large display settings where its jewel-like inlines and ball terminals can read clearly.
description: Rooted in early nineteenth-century Didone conventions, Zina pairs light, lightly bracketed serifs with prominent ball terminals and decorative inline strokes that run through the thick parts of each letterform. That inline treatment is the typeface's defining characteristic, giving letters a faceted, cut-glass quality that only reads well at subheadline sizes and above. Available in one static style with coverage for 135 languages across an extended Latin character set.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Zina as a stately and elegant inline serif face built for display use, drawing on early nineteenth-century Didone design. Its light serifs are only slightly bracketed, several letters carry prominent ball terminals, and inline strokes running through each thick stroke give the face a jewel-like quality. It is recommended for subheadline sizes or larger.

name: Tabular
classification_notes: Every glyph shares the same advance width, making it a true monospaced family; the letterforms are clean and sans-serif in character, positioned explicitly for code display and tabular data.
summary: A monospaced sans from Indian Type Foundry spanning five weights with italics, built for code display, financial data and onscreen text.
description: Every glyph shares a fixed advance width, so line length stays constant when switching between weights or italics, a property that makes it genuinely useful for coding environments and financial layouts. Five weights run from Light to Bold, each with a companion italic, and two variable fonts cover the full weight axis. The 135-language Latin coverage and ITF Free Font Licence make it a practical choice for international web and app work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Tabular as a family of ten monospaced sans serif fonts across five weights with companion italics, where every glyph shares the same advance width so that line-wrap is unaffected by emboldening or italicising. They recommend it for onscreen use in apps and on the web, particularly wherever code must be displayed, noting that monospaced families with italics are rare. It also performs well in print for financial reporting or letterheads featuring multiple telephone numbers.

name: Paquito
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises it as Serif, Slab. The letterforms are condensed and whimsically hand-lettered in character, placing it firmly in display slab territory with intentional irregularity and quirk.
summary: A condensed, whimsically drawn display slab by Juanjo Lopez, released through Indian Type Foundry, with a hand-lettered irregularity suited to headlines and food-sector design.
description: Three weights span Regular to Bold, all drawn with a fine-tipped pen character that introduces deliberate irregularity across the letterforms. The condensed proportions make it efficient at large sizes, and details such as top-heavy uppercase letters, unconventional numerals and 46 ligatures reward close inspection. Paquito works well in editorial headlines but finds a natural home in restaurant identities, menu design and food packaging.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Paquito as a small family of serif fonts for display sizes, whimsically drawn by Juanjo Lopez. The letterforms are condensed and appear hand-lettered with a fine-tipped pen, producing intentional irregularity. The fonts include 46 ligatures, case-sensitive punctuation and localised Polish diacritics, and are recommended for editorial work and the gastronomic sector, including restaurant identities, menu-setting and food packaging.

name: Expose
