classification_notes: Described explicitly as a constructed sans, Expose draws from engineered letterforms and avant-garde typewriter aesthetics, with squared counters and redirected diagonals that give it a fixed-width feel despite proportional spacing.
summary: A constructed sans by Indian Type Foundry that blends engineered letterforms with typewriter aesthetics, aimed at headlines and editorial work.
description: Expose draws on letterforms that feel drafted by engineers, then crossed with avant-garde takes on typewriter faces. Its counters are almost squared off and several diagonal strokes redirect near one end, giving the design a fixed-width quality despite being proportionally spaced. An unusual double-storied 'g' adds a subtle quirk to an otherwise rigorously structured family of four weights, available as static fonts and a variable weight axis.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Expose as a constructed sans typeface for headlines and general editorial work, noting that it looks as though letterforms drafted by engineers have been crossed with avant-garde remixes of typewriter faces. The counters inside letters are almost squared off, and several diagonal strokes change direction towards one end, making the design feel somewhat fixed-width despite being proportionally spaced. Unusually for a constructed-style sans, it uses a double-storied 'g'.

name: Kihim
classification_notes: Categorised explicitly as Display by Fontshare; a single-weight decorative typeface built on an extreme diagonal grid with rotalic letterforms, making it purely ornamental and unsuited to any text role.
summary: A single-weight display typeface by Hitesh Malaviya built on an extreme diagonal grid, conceived as a typographic homage to Indian artist Nasreen Mohamedi.
description: Every letterform in Kihim is set on a sharp clockwise angle, with stroke terminals that echo that tilt to produce a rotalic effect as though each character has been physically rotated. The design draws directly from Mohamedi's grid-based drawings, in which intervals between lines carry as much weight as the lines themselves. At small sizes text can appear abstract or illegible; as the eye adjusts to the rhythm of thick and thin diagonals, the letterforms resolve, much as Mohamedi's own compositions reward sustained looking.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Kihim as a single-weight display typeface with a grid-based, highly decorative construction in which all letterforms are set on an extreme angle. Designed by Hitesh Malaviya, it is his interpretation of late artist Nasreen Mohamedi's drawings and photographs, whose work explored light, shadow and structured grids. OpenType Contextual Alternates trigger intriguing glyph repositioning, and oldstyle figures are included as an optional feature. The typeface is named after the place where Mohamedi died in 1990.

name: Segment
classification_notes: Categorised as Display by the foundry; all letterforms are constructed from discrete segments mimicking LCD electronic display technology, with a monospaced, caps-only structure that places it firmly in the novelty display category.
summary: A caps-only, monospaced display face by Oslo-based designer Frode Helland, built entirely from discrete segments in the manner of LCD electronic displays but with unexpectedly old-fashioned serif detailing.
description: Every letterform is constructed from segments, yet the design departs from a purely utilitarian LCD aesthetic through the inclusion of serifs, a sweeping diagonal tail on the capital R, and a wavy stroke on the top of the 7. The font is caps-only and monospaced, with lowercase slots filled by uppercase duplicates, and includes over a dozen currency symbols alongside two OpenType Stylistic Sets offering alternate punctuation. Despite the late-twentieth-century technology it references, the overall character is eclectic and archaic rather than coldly digital.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Segment as an LCD-style display font from Oslo-based designer Frode Helland, noting that its letterforms are all constructed from segments. They suggest it for poster design and identity work for businesses in transit-connected cities, as well as for headlines on digital subjects. They highlight its unusual serifs for an experimental display font, the old-fashioned detailing on numerals and currency symbols, its monospaced caps-only structure, and the inclusion of five base grid glyphs, multiple currency symbols, and two Stylistic Sets for alternate punctuation.

name: New Title
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises it as Sans and Display; the narrow, near-monolinear letterforms and utilitarian character make it a compressed display sans aimed at titling contexts rather than body text.
summary: A narrow, near-monolinear display sans designed by Indian Type Foundry for film title sequences and credits work.
description: Built around compressed letterforms with almost monolinear strokes, New Title suits the precise, utilitarian demands of screen titles and credits sequences. Distinctive details include a capital J whose out-stroke doesn't descend, two forms of the lowercase g, and currency symbols with a gap through their main strokes. The family runs across five static weights from Extralight to Bold, plus a variable font, covering 135 languages across Latin Extended.
foundry_blurb: New Title is described by Indian Type Foundry as a typeface family designed specifically for film titles and credits sequences. Its narrow, utilitarian design relies on near-monolinear strokes, with visible contrast appearing only in select characters. Notable design choices include a non-descending capital J whose terminal mirrors those on C, G, S and several numerals, two forms of the lowercase g, and currency symbols with a gap in the centre of their main strokes.

name: Striper
classification_notes: Categorised as Display by the foundry; Striper is a decorative sans serif where straight strokes are replaced with zebra-stripe patterns, making it unsuitable for text use and firmly ornamental in character.
summary: A decorative sans serif from Indian Type Foundry in which every straight stroke is replaced with a zebra-stripe pattern, intended for large-size headline use.
description: Designed by Jitka Janečková, Striper swaps the straight horizontal, vertical and diagonal strokes of a conventional sans serif for a repeating stripe motif, giving single words or short headlines a distinctive hand-crafted ornamental quality. The font ships with two alternate forms per character via OpenType Stylistic Sets: the first offers different skeletal shapes and alternative stripe placements, while the second provides significantly wider letterforms that function almost as a second extended variant within the same file. The stripes are sized for display use, and the effect loses legibility at small sizes.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Striper as a decorative sans serif in which straight strokes throughout the character set have been replaced with a zebra-stripe pattern. They note it works best at large sizes, set as single words or short headlines rather than running text, and that its two OpenType Stylistic Sets offer alternative skeletal forms and a much wider set of letterforms respectively.

name: Pilcrow Rounded
classification_notes: Square-like proportions with rounded terminals and a utilitarian, signage-derived construction place this firmly in the geometric sans category, with mechanical regularity across all weights.
summary: A rounded, utilitarian sans from Indian Type Foundry, distributed free exclusively through Fontshare, drawing on the visual logic of street signage and industrial lettering.
description: Square-ish proportions and clean rounded terminals give Pilcrow Rounded a mechanical, signage-derived quality that reads well at small sizes on screen. The family runs from Regular to Heavy across five static weights plus a variable font, with no italics by design, the expectation being that weight contrast carries hierarchical emphasis instead. It sits within the broader Pilcrow family, of which Pilcrow and Pilcrow Soft are commercial releases, while this rounded variant is distributed free via Fontshare.
foundry_blurb: Indian Type Foundry describe Pilcrow Rounded as simple and utilitarian, its forms referencing street and highway signage and cast-metal industrial lettering. Glyphs have square-like proportions that are proportionally spaced, with widths increasing slightly as weight increases. The family deliberately omits italics, relying on weight contrast for emphasis. ITF note its suitability for short screen texts, news tickers and tech-style branding, citing the clean letterforms' legibility on digital interfaces.

name: Anton
classification_notes: Fontshare categorises Anton as Sans, Display; it is a reworked advertising sans serif with letterforms reshaped for bold display use, with opened counters and optimised stems rather than general text setting.
summary: A reworked advertising sans serif by Vernon Adams, digitised and reshaped with opened counters for bold display use on the web.
description: Vernon Adams took a traditional advertising sans as his starting point, then re-digitised and reshaped the letterforms for screen, opening counters slightly and optimising stems for bold display rendering in modern browsers. Only a single Regular weight is available, but the design is unapologetically heavy and suited to headlines and banners. Licensed under the SIL Open Font License and distributed freely via Fontshare.
foundry_blurb: Anton is described as a reworking of a traditional advertising sans serif typeface, with letterforms digitised and reshaped for web use. The counters have been opened up and the stems optimised to function as a bold display font in modern web browsers.

name: Nunito
designer: Vernon Adams, Cyreal, Jacques Le Bailly
classification_notes: A sans-serif with consistently rounded terminals throughout, giving it a soft, friendly character distinct from geometric or grotesque styles. Designed primarily as a display face but functional at text sizes too.
summary: A rounded sans-serif with soft terminals across eight weights, designed for display use but versatile enough for body text on screen.
description: Nunito features consistently rounded terminals that give it a warm, approachable quality across its 16 static styles and two variable fonts. Designed by Vernon Adams and later extended by Cyreal and Jacques Le Bailly, it covers Extra Light through Black with matching italics. Its balance of friendliness and legibility makes it a reliable choice for interfaces, editorial headlines and web typography.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Nunito as a well-balanced sans-serif with rounded terminals, designed primarily as a display font but suitable for text use too. It is licensed under the SIL Open Font License and intended for free use across desktop, laptop and mobile web browsers.

name: Kohinoor Zerone
classification_notes: Classified as Display by the foundry; the family comprises two conceptual extremes, a hairline-stroke font and a single-stroke skeleton font, both rooted in a geometric humanist sans structure intended for headlines and lettering rather than body text.
summary: Two conceptual display cuts from ITF's Kohinoor Multiscript lineage: One, claimed as the world's thinnest retail font at a single-unit stroke width, and Zero, a stroke-skeleton tool for designers to apply their own outlines.
description: Kohinoor One pushes stroke weight to its absolute minimum at one unit thick, making it a specimen of extreme hairline design within ITF's long-running Kohinoor Multiscript superfamily. Kohinoor Zero works differently: its glyphs are rendered as closed outlines that simulate single strokes, intended to be converted to paths and stripped below the descender line so designers can apply custom brushes or outlines in Illustrator or InDesign. The concept was first presented at TypeCon 2010 by Satya Rajpurohit, giving these two idiosyncratic cuts a longer development history than most display releases.
foundry_blurb: ITF describe Kohinoor One as the world's thinnest retail font, with strokes just one unit wide, and position it as an addition to Kohinoor Multiscript, their oldest superfamily spanning over 150 languages. Kohinoor Zero is framed as a tool font for designers: its closed outlines simulate single strokes, and users convert text to paths and delete nodes below the descender line to produce a skeleton they can dress with any stroke or brush of their choosing.

name: Poppins
classification_notes: Poppins is explicitly described as a geometric sans serif, with nearly monolinear letterforms and more constructed, rationalist glyphs than is typical of humanist or grotesque designs.
summary: A geometric sans serif by Indian Type Foundry with nearly monolinear strokes and 18 static styles spanning Thin to Black, available as a variable font.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry, Poppins sits within the long tradition of geometric sans serifs, characterised by constructed, rationalist letterforms and near-monolinear stroke contrast. Optical corrections are applied at stroke joints to preserve even typographic colour across the weight range. The family covers 18 static styles plus two variable fonts, making it broadly applicable across screen interfaces, corporate communications and branding.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Poppins as a newcomer to the geometric sans serif tradition, with letterforms that are more constructed and rationalist than is typical. Each glyph is nearly monolinear, with optical corrections at stroke joints to maintain even typographic colour. The typeface is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Lora
classification_notes: A contemporary text serif with calligraphic roots, moderate stroke contrast and bracketed serifs, placing it between humanist and transitional classifications.
summary: A contemporary text serif by Olga Karpushina and Alexei Vanyashin for Cyreal, combining calligraphic brushed curves with upright driving serifs for body text use.
description: Designed by Olga Karpushina and Alexei Vanyashin and released in 2011, Lora pairs brushed, calligraphic curves with firm, contrasting serifs to give running text a distinct but unobtrusive character. Eight static styles run from Regular to Bold with matching italics, alongside variable font versions. Optimised for screen while performing equally well in print, it suits editorial, publishing and long-form digital work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Lora as a well-balanced contemporary serif with roots in calligraphy, offering moderate contrast and suitability for body text. They note that a paragraph set in Lora makes a memorable appearance through its brushed curves in contrast with driving serifs, conveying the mood of a modern-day story or art essay. Designed by Olga Karpushina and Alexei Vanyashin for Cyreal, it is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Khand
classification_notes: Khand is described as a mono-linear sans built on a modular system with open counter forms, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition. Its compact, display-optimised proportions distinguish it from humanist or grotesque classifications.
summary: A compact, modular mono-linear sans designed for display and headline use, built with open counters and shortened vowel marks to allow tight line-setting.
description: Designed by Satya Rajpurohit at Indian Type Foundry and first published in 2014, Khand uses a modular construction in which letterforms share strong formal commonality without becoming repetitive. The base character height runs large on the body, with vowel marks deliberately abbreviated to permit close vertical spacing in headline settings. Five weights from Light to Bold cover both short text paragraphs and single-word display applications, and a variable font is also available.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Khand as a family of compact mono-linear fonts with very open counter forms, developed primarily for headline and display typography. The modular design system gives the letterforms a dynamic consistency, while shortened vowel marks reduce interlinear space for tighter headline setting. The lighter weights suit short running text, while the heavier styles are optimised for single-word or headline use. Khand is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Quicksand
classification_notes: Quicksand is explicitly described as a geometric sans serif, built on geometric shapes as its core structural foundation, designed primarily for display use.
summary: A geometric sans with rounded terminals by Andrew Paglinawan, originally drawn in 2008 for display use but readable at smaller sizes.
description: Built on geometric shapes from the outset, Quicksand carries soft, rounded terminals that give it a friendly, approachable tone suited to branding, fashion and headline work. Five static weights from Light to Bold are available alongside a variable font, offering flexibility across a range of applications. The family is released under the SIL Open Font License and distributed freely via Fontshare.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Quicksand as a sans serif typeface designed by Andrew Paglinawan in 2008, using geometric shapes as its core foundation. It is intended for display purposes while remaining legible at smaller sizes, and the family includes five styles with variable font support under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Asap
classification_notes: Asap is a contemporary sans-serif with subtly rounded corners and standardised character widths across styles, placing it in the humanist sans tradition with a friendly, screen-optimised character.
summary: A contemporary sans-serif with subtly rounded corners and a fixed character width across all styles, so changing weights never reflows the text.
description: Designed by Pablo Cosgaya and developed with Andrés Torresi, Nicolás Silva and Yorlmar Campos, Asap is based on the earlier typeface Ancha. Its defining feature is standardised character widths across all eight static styles, allowing weight changes mid-composition without disrupting line lengths. Available as both static and variable fonts under the SIL Open Font Licence, it is oriented towards screen and desktop use.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Asap as a contemporary sans-serif family with subtle rounded corners, specially developed for screen and desktop use. Its standardised character width across all styles means lines of text remain the same length when switching weights, letting users change type styles without reflowing a text body. The family is licensed under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Crimson Pro
classification_notes: Crimson Pro follows in the tradition of old-style humanist serifs inspired by the work of Renaissance type designers, with high contrast strokes and angled stress suited to extended reading.
summary: A Google-commissioned redesign of the original Crimson typeface by Jacques Le Bailly, refined for colour, glyph balance and legibility across 16 static styles and two variable fonts.
description: Designed by Jacques Le Bailly (@Fonthausen), Crimson Pro is a thorough reworking of the original Crimson, itself drawn in the Renaissance old-style tradition. The redesign addresses shortcomings in glyph balance and on-screen legibility through months of revision, resulting in a family that runs from Extra Light to Black with matching italics throughout. Available as a variable font and released under the SIL Open Font License, it suits magazine, publishing and long-form editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Crimson Pro as a professionally produced redesign of Crimson by Jacques Le Bailly, commissioned by Google. They note it as a fresh take on the original, the result of months of painstaking work to perfect colour, glyph balance and legibility, and released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Space Grotesk
classification_notes: A proportional grotesque sans derived from Space Mono, retaining monospace-influenced quirks while optimising for text legibility. Its idiosyncratic letterforms and fixed-width origins give it a distinctive technical character within the grotesque category.
summary: A proportional grotesque drawn from Colophon Foundry's Space Mono, preserving its fixed-width quirks while improving readability at text sizes.
description: Florian Karsten derived Space Grotesk from Space Mono in 2018, translating the monospace family's distinctive letterform details into a proportional format suited to body and UI text. Five static weights from Light to Bold are available alongside a variable font, with coverage spanning Latin Vietnamese, Pinyin and most European scripts. OpenType features include old-style and tabular figures, superscript and subscript numerals, fractions and stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Space Grotesk as a proportional sans-serif typeface variant based on Colophon Foundry's fixed-width Space Mono family from 2016. Originally designed by Florian Karsten in 2018, it retains the monospace's idiosyncratic details while optimising for readability at non-display sizes, and supports Latin Vietnamese, Pinyin and all Western, Central and South-Eastern European languages.

name: Literata
classification_notes: A text serif designed for long-form reading, originally commissioned by Google Play Books, with optical qualities suited to print body text — low contrast, generous x-height and open apertures typical of contemporary book serifs.
summary: A text serif by TypeTogether, originally commissioned for Google Play Books, offered here in its print variant across eight weights with matching italics and variable axes.
description: Designed by TypeTogether for Google Play Books, Literata exists in two versions — one for screen-based ebooks and one for print — and this is the print release. Sixteen static styles span Extra Light to Black with matching italics, alongside two variable fonts covering weight and its italic counterpart. With 1,761 glyphs and an open-source licence, it is a capable choice for long-form text work where legibility and weight range matter.
foundry_blurb: Literata is designed by TypeTogether and was commissioned for Google Play Books. Two versions of the family exist, one for print and one for ebooks; this is the print version. It is licensed under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Sora
classification_notes: Sora is a geometric sans with a large x-height and open counters, designed with low-resolution screen aesthetics in mind. Its neutral, constructed letterforms place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition, optimised for UI contexts.
summary: A geometric sans commissioned for the SORA blockchain economy, designed by Jonathan Barnbrook and Julián Moncada for UI and branding use.
description: Originally a custom typeface for the SORAMITSU-developed SORA decentralised economy, the family draws on low-resolution screen typography without sentimentality, reworking those cues for contemporary digital environments. A generous x-height and open counters make it well suited to app and web interfaces where legibility at small sizes matters. Eight weights with matching italics and variable font support give it practical range for product and branding work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Sora as a typeface commissioned for the SORA decentralised autonomous economy, designed for branding and interface use by Jonathan Barnbrook and Julián Moncada. They note that SORAMITSU, a Japanese blockchain technology company, inspired the design's spirit and heritage, with the outcome being a neutral yet distinctive sans serif featuring a large x-height and generous counters, highly optimised for app and web interfaces.

name: Hind
classification_notes: Hind is described as having humanist-style construction with monolinear strokes and open apertures, placing it firmly in the humanist sans category despite its UI-focused intent.
summary: A humanist sans from Indian Type Foundry built for UI work, with monolinear strokes, flat terminals and open apertures across five weights.
description: Designed with user interfaces in mind, Hind pairs humanist letterform construction with predominantly monolinear strokes that terminate horizontally or vertically rather than on a diagonal. This approach creates clean counter forms and contributes to strong legibility at small sizes. Available in five static weights plus a variable font, it is released under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: Hind is an open source typeface family developed explicitly for use in user interface design. Its letterforms combine humanist construction with seemingly monolinear strokes that end with horizontal or vertical shears rather than diagonal cuts, producing clear counter forms and open apertures that make the family highly legible when setting text.

name: Rajdhani
classification_notes: Rajdhani uses modularised, squared letterforms with flat terminals ending on horizontals or verticals rather than diagonals, giving it a geometric, condensed character suited to display use.
summary: A modularised geometric sans from Indian Type Foundry, built around squared bowls and flat terminals for headline and display use on screen.
description: Designed by Indian Type Foundry, Rajdhani replaces conventionally round letterform elements with straight sides and flat stroke terminals that end on horizontals or verticals rather than diagonals. The slightly rounded corners soften what would otherwise be a severe technical character, lending the face a quietly futuristic quality. Five weights from Light to Bold are available as static fonts alongside a variable weight axis.
foundry_blurb: Rajdhani is an open source typeface intended for headlines and display-sized text on screen. Its letterforms are modularised, with a squared and condensed appearance that reads as technical or futuristic. Round bowls are replaced with straight-sided equivalents, and stroke terminals end flat on horizontals or verticals rather than diagonals, with slightly rounded corners to keep the overall feeling from turning sharp.

name: Azeret Mono
classification_notes: A mono-linear monospaced typeface with roots in OCR fonts and operating system interfaces; the fixed-pitch spacing and sans construction place it firmly in the mono category.
summary: A monospaced typeface from Displaay drawing on OCR fonts and nineties operating system aesthetics, released as open source through Fontshare.
description: Designed by Displaay, Martin Vácha and Daniel Quisek, Azeret Mono is rooted in an exploration of OCR typefaces, retro operating systems and screen interfaces. The result is a mono-linear monospaced family with a character that sits somewhere between utilitarian and personality-driven, suited to coding environments and UI work. Eighteen static styles span Thin to Black with matching italics, and two variable fonts cover the weight axis.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Azeret Mono as a monospaced typeface with a mono-linear character, its origins traced to an exploration of OCR fonts, past and futuristic operating systems, various interfaces and the nineties. The final design is driven by a desire to achieve an appearance suited to operating systems, combining those influences with details that evoke a specific personality. It is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Dancing Script
classification_notes: A lively casual script with bouncing letterforms and size variation; Fontshare categorises it as Script, referencing 1950s casual scripts such as Murray Hill and Mistral.
summary: A casual script by Pablo Impallari with bouncing letterforms and variable cap heights, drawing on 1950s reference points such as Mistral and Murray Hill.
description: Letters shift in size and sit above and below the baseline to mimic the spontaneous rhythm of informal handwriting. The design draws weight distribution from Emil Klumpp's Murray Hill (1956) and the lively bounce of Roger Excoffon's Mistral (1953). Available in Regular and Bold static styles plus a variable weight axis, it suits posters, packaging and beauty branding where a friendly, unpolished tone is needed.
foundry_blurb: Dancing Script is described by Fontshare as a lively casual script where letters bounce and change size slightly, with large caps that dip below the baseline. It references popular script typefaces from the 1950s, specifically Murray Hill for weight distribution and Mistral for its bouncing effect. Designed by Pablo Impallari of Impallari Type and released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Roundo
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans serif with humanist influences in the lowercase. Capital letters are more purely geometric while lowercase blends geometric and humanist features, placing it firmly in the geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A geometric sans with a top-heavy reverse contrast and a lowercase that blends geometric and humanist features, designed at the Indian Type Foundry for headline and screen use.
description: Designed by Shiva Nallaperumal at the Indian Type Foundry and first published in 2016, Roundo pairs more purely geometric capitals with a lowercase that mixes geometric and humanist sans conventions. The reverse contrast gives characters a top-heavy quality, while diagonal shears on ascenders and round dots throughout add energy. Six static weights from Extra Light to Bold are accompanied by a variable font axis, with each style covering 408 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Roundo is described as a geometric sans serif typeface optimised for headlines and on-screen display text. Its character set features top-heavy letters with reverse contrast most visible in the lowercase, which hybridises geometric and humanist sans features. Capitals are more purely geometric, the x-height is tall, and ascenders carry diagonal shears for dynamism. All dots are round. The family comprises six static styles and a variable font, each containing 408 glyphs, and is licensed under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Teko
classification_notes: Teko is a display-oriented sans-serif with low stroke contrast and square proportions, designed explicitly for headlines and large-scale screen text rather than body copy.
summary: An open-source display sans by Manushi Parikh at the Indian Type Foundry, built for large-scale headlines with low stroke contrast and square proportions.
description: Designed at the Indian Type Foundry and first published in 2016, Teko targets headlines, news tickers and advertising copy where legibility at large sizes matters more than text performance. Its letterforms carry low stroke contrast and a visually simple, squarish structure that reads cleanly on screen. Light through Bold weights give flexibility across headline hierarchies, with the variable font covering the full range in a single file.
foundry_blurb: Teko is an open-source typeface created for headlines and display-sized text on screen. It features low stroke contrast, square proportions and a visually simple structure. ITF note that display families with extensive character sets are rare for Indic scripts, and Teko is one of their exceptions. Regular, Medium and Semi Bold suit long headlines; Bold is best reserved for one or two words; and Light excels in large web headlines. It works equally well in print and on screen, and is suited to advertising and television news tickers.

name: Kalam
classification_notes: Kalam is explicitly categorised as a handwriting-style font, with letterforms derived from casual everyday handwriting made with a thin felt-tip or ball-point pen. Script is the appropriate top-level category, with handwriting as the subcategory.
summary: A handwriting-style family from Indian Type Foundry, optimised for on-screen legibility with letterforms imitating a felt-tip or ball-point pen.
description: Kalam draws its letterforms from casual everyday handwriting, featuring a steep slant from top right to bottom left that gives it a fresh, personal quality on screen and in print. Three static weights run from Light to Bold, with a variable axis covering the full range. Despite its informal origins, the family has been tuned for text rendering on screen rather than left as a raw digitisation of handwriting.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Kalam as a handwriting-style font family designed for personal communication, with letterforms optimised for text on screen. They note that letters resemble those written with a thin felt-tip or ball-point pen, featuring a steep slant, and that the result feels personal and fresh whether seen on screen or printed on the page.

name: Merriweather Sans
classification_notes: Described as a low-contrast semi-condensed sans-serif with traditional feeling despite modern screen-oriented shapes, placing it in the humanist sans tradition rather than geometric or grotesque.
summary: A low-contrast, semi-condensed sans-serif by Eben Sorkin, built for legibility at small sizes on screen while retaining a traditional feel.
description: Designed by Eben Sorkin of Sorkin Type, it pairs modern screen-friendly letterforms with a warmth and rhythm more typical of traditional text faces. The family spans six weights from Light to Extra Bold, each with a matching italic, and is available as a variable font. Its Latin script coverage and open-source licence under the SIL OFL make it a practical choice for digital publishing and UI work.
foundry_blurb: Merriweather Sans is described as a low-contrast semi-condensed sans-serif family designed for readability at very small sizes. Despite adopting modern shapes suited to screens, it retains a traditional feeling. It supports languages using the Latin script and its variants, and is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Plus Jakarta Sans
classification_notes: Described as a geometric sans-serif drawing on Neuzeit Grotesk, Futura and 1930s grotesque references, with near-monoline contrast and pointy curves characteristic of the geometric tradition.
summary: A geometric sans-serif originally commissioned for the Jakarta Provincial Government's +Jakarta City of Collaboration identity in 2020, later released as an open-source family.
description: Designed by Gumpita Rahayu at Tokotype, it draws on Neuzeit Grotesk, Futura and 1930s grotesque sans-serifs, with near-monoline contrast and a slightly taller x-height for legibility across sizes. Three stylistic alternates, named Lancip (Sharp), Lurus (Straight) and Lingkar (Swirl), give individual glyphs distinctive character. The family spans seven weights with matching italics and includes variable font files for both weight and italic axes.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Plus Jakarta Sans as a geometric sans-serif designed by Gumpita Rahayu of Tokotype, originally commissioned by 6616 Studio for the Jakarta Provincial Government's +Jakarta City of Collaboration identity programme in 2020. The design takes inspiration from Neuzeit Grotesk, Futura and 1930s grotesque sans-serifs, featuring near-monoline contrast, pointy curves and a taller x-height for legibility. Each style includes stylistic alternates divided into three sets: Lancip (Sharp), Lurus (Straight) and Lingkar (Swirl), released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Fira Sans
classification_notes: Fira Sans is a humanist sans-serif originally designed to complement the Mozilla FirefoxOS interface, balancing screen legibility with warm, readable letterforms suited to UI and editorial contexts.
summary: An open-source humanist sans originally commissioned for Mozilla FirefoxOS, covering nine weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Designed by Carrois Apostrophe in Berlin to integrate with the visual character of Mozilla FirefoxOS, Fira Sans prioritises legibility across a wide range of screen qualities and handset rendering conditions. Eighteen static styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, complemented by two variable font files covering the full weight axis. Its balanced, readable letterforms make it a practical choice for UI, branding and editorial applications.
foundry_blurb: Fira Sans was designed to integrate with the character of Mozilla FirefoxOS, while also covering legibility needs across a large range of handsets varying in screen quality and rendering. The project is led by Carrois, a type foundry based in Berlin, and is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: JetBrains Mono
classification_notes: Explicitly designed for developers and bundled with JetBrains IDEs; fixed-width letterforms optimised for code readability with OpenType ligature support, placing it firmly in the code monospace subcategory.
summary: A developer-focused monospace family designed by JetBrains, covering eight weights with italics and variable font support, bundled with JetBrains IDEs.
description: Built specifically for coding environments, the family ships with OpenType ligatures that can be toggled off for IDEs lacking OpenType support, with a separate NL variant provided for that purpose. Eight weights from Thin to Extra Bold each have matching italics, and two variable font files cover the full weight axis. Licensed under the SIL Open Font License and freely available as a standalone download.
foundry_blurb: JetBrains Mono is a typeface family designed for developers, available as a standalone download and bundled with all JetBrains IDEs. It ships with OpenType features and ligatures, which can be toggled on or off in settings; a separate NL version without ligatures is provided for IDEs that lack OpenType support. The project is licensed under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Red Hat Display
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a geometric sans taking inspiration from American sans serifs including Tempo and Highway Gothic, with low contrast, large x-height and open counters optimised for display use.
summary: Originally commissioned by Paula Scher of Pentagram, this geometric sans was designed for Red Hat's corporate identity and optimised for headlines and large-scale use.
description: Designed by Jeremy Mickel of MCKL, Red Hat Display draws from American sans serifs such as Tempo and Highway Gothic, resulting in a low-contrast geometric with a large x-height and tight spacing suited to headlines. It forms part of a wider superfamily alongside Text and Mono styles, with the Display cut distinguished by its open counters and close-set characters. Available in seven weights with matching italics and variable font support, it is released under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: Red Hat Display is part of a superfamily of Display, Text, and Mono styles commissioned by Paula Scher of Pentagram and designed by Jeremy Mickel of MCKL for the Red Hat brand identity. The Display styles are low contrast and tightly spaced with a large x-height and open counters, taking inspiration from American sans serifs including Tempo and Highway Gothic, and are intended for headlines and large-scale settings.

name: Oswald
classification_notes: Oswald is explicitly described as a reworking of the classic gothic typeface style, referencing Alternate Gothic as a direct precedent. Its condensed, upright letterforms place it firmly in the American gothic sans tradition.
summary: A reworking of the Alternate Gothic tradition, redrawn for screen legibility across six weights with variable font support.
description: Designed by Vernon Adams, Kalapi Gajjar and Cyreal, Oswald takes the condensed uppercase-heavy rhythm of American gothic sans-serifs and redraws it for digital screens at standard pixel densities. Six static weights run from Extra Light to Bold, with a variable font covering the full range. It is built for headline and display use across web, poster and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Oswald as a reworking of the classic gothic typeface style historically represented by designs such as Alternate Gothic. The characters have been redrawn to better fit the pixel grid of standard digital screens, and the typeface is intended to be used freely across the internet by web browsers on desktop, laptop and mobile devices. It is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Work Sans
classification_notes: Described as loosely based on early Grotesques from foundries such as Stephenson Blake and Bauerschen Giesserei, with simplified features optimised for screen rendering across a wide weight range.
summary: An open-source grotesque by Wei Huang, drawn from early foundry grotesques and optimised for on-screen use across nine weights.
description: Loosely referencing Stephenson Blake and Bauerschen Giesserei grotesques, Work Sans is tuned for screen legibility at medium sizes, with simplified features and enlarged diacritic marks suited to digital rendering. The nine static weights span Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and the family is also available as a variable font. Heavier and lighter cuts lean towards display use, while the middle weights hold up well for body text from 14px upward.
foundry_blurb: Work Sans is a typeface family based loosely on early Grotesques from Stephenson Blake, Miller & Richard and Bauerschen Giesserei. Middle weights are optimised for on-screen text at medium sizes and work in print too, while the extreme weights are designed for display use on web and in print. Features are simplified for screen resolution, with larger diacritic marks than would appear in print. Designed by Wei Huang, an Australian type designer, and released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Outfit
designer: On Brand Investments Pty Ltd, Rodrigo Fuenzalida
classification_notes: Outfit is categorised as Sans on Fontshare; its clean, open forms and even stroke contrast place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition, designed for screen and headline use.
summary: A geometric sans-serif created as the official typeface for brand automation platform Outfit.io, released as an open-source family with nine weights and a variable font.
description: Designed by Rodrigo Fuenzalida for On Brand Investments Pty Ltd, the typeface draws inspiration from the ligature-rich Outfit.io wordmark and was built to connect the company's written voice to its product identity. Nine static weights run from Thin to Black, accompanied by a variable font axis, making it adaptable across screen, headline and packaging contexts. Released under the SIL Open Font License in May 2022, it is a workmanlike geometric sans with broad weight coverage but limited stylistic distinction.
foundry_blurb: Outfit is the official typeface for brand automation company Outfit.io, inspired by the ligature-rich Outfit wordmark. Designed to link Outfit's written voice to its product marks, the family is released under the SIL Open Font License with nine static styles and a variable font, and is described as 'on brand by default'.

name: Karma
classification_notes: Karma tends toward the monolinear in construction with a tall x-height and economic character widths, placing it closer to humanist serif territory designed primarily for screen body text legibility.
summary: A screen-oriented serif by Joana Correia for Indian Type Foundry, built around monolinear construction, a tall x-height and economical character widths.
description: Designed by Joana Correia and first published by Indian Type Foundry in 2014, Karma was conceived specifically for body text on screen. Its characters tend toward the monolinear and are kept narrow, with a tall x-height that aids legibility at small sizes. Five static weights from Light to Bold are available alongside a variable font, and the family is released under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describe Karma as developed for body text on screen, with characters that feature a monolinear construction style, economic widths and a tall x-height. Joana Correia designed it for Indian Type Foundry, who first published it in 2014. It is available under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Familjen Grotesk
designer: Kristian Möller, Anders Wikström, Jonas Bäckman, Matilda Gysing, Örjan Nordling
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly places it within the grotesque subgenre, citing large x-height, closed apertures and sturdy uppercase letterforms as key characteristics.
summary: A contemporary grotesque from Stockholm studio Familjen STHLM AB, distinguished by prominent ink traps that add character at display sizes whilst aiding clarity at small text sizes.
description: Designed by a team led by Kristian Möller, this sans serif sits comfortably in the grotesque tradition with a large x-height, closed apertures and sturdy uppercase forms. Ink traps cut visibly into the joins, giving it a distinctive edge at headline sizes without disrupting readability in body copy. Available in four weights with matching italics plus variable font axes, and released under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: Familjen Grotesk is described as a sans serif with a contemporary appearance suited to both text and display use. Large ink traps add style to headlines and clarity to captions, while the large x-height, closed apertures and sturdy uppercase letters align it with the grotesque subgenre. The project is led by Kristian Möller, with the Latin component designed by a team at Familjen STHLM AB in Stockholm.

name: Public Sans
designer: Pablo Impallari, Rodrigo Fuenzalida, Dan Williams, USWDS
classification_notes: Derived from Libre Franklin, itself rooted in the American grotesque tradition; designed explicitly for neutral, utilitarian performance across interfaces and text.
summary: A neutral grotesque developed by the United States Web Design System, derived from Libre Franklin and built for interfaces and running text.
description: Commissioned by the US Web Design System and based on Libre Franklin, this is a no-frills grotesque aimed squarely at digital interfaces and government web properties. The family runs to nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, plus two variable font files covering the full weight range. At 18 static styles and a clean 648-glyph set, it covers standard Latin requirements without ornamentation.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Public Sans as a strong, neutral typeface for interfaces, text and headings, developed by the United States Web Design System and based on Libre Franklin. It is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Manrope
classification_notes: Manrope is a modern sans-serif with clean geometric proportions and a rational structure, placing it squarely in the geometric sans category without strong humanist warmth or grotesque irregularity.
summary: A clean geometric sans by Mikhail Sharanda, spanning seven weights with a variable font option, suited to screen interfaces and branding.
description: Designed between 2018 and 2021, Manrope offers seven static weights from Extra Light to Extra Bold alongside a variable font covering the full weight range. With 807 glyphs and a rational, modern structure, it performs well at both display and text sizes on screen. Released under the SIL Open Font License and distributed through Fontshare, it is a practical choice for apps, websites and logo work.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Manrope as a modern sans-serif font family designed by Mikhail Sharanda between 2018 and 2021, available in seven static styles plus a variable font, licensed under the SIL Open Font License and suited to apps, banners, logos and websites.

name: Archivo
classification_notes: Archivo is explicitly described as a grotesque sans serif, drawing on late nineteenth century American typeface traditions with characteristics suited to both print and screen use.
summary: A grotesque sans from Omnibus-Type drawing on late nineteenth century American typefaces, offered in 18 static styles and two variable fonts with support for over 200 languages.
description: Designed originally for headlines and highlights, Archivo carries the proportions and rhythm of American grotesques from the 1890s while being optimised for both print and screen. The family runs from Thin to Black with matching italics across all nine weights, plus variable font files for weight axes. Its 834-glyph set and broad language coverage make it practical for multilingual digital publishing.
foundry_blurb: Archivo is described as a grotesque sans serif originally designed for highlights and headlines, reminiscent of late nineteenth century American typefaces. Its technical and aesthetic characteristics are crafted for high performance typography, intended for simultaneous use in print and online platforms, and supports over 200 world languages. It is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Spline Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a Grotesque, with condensed proportions derived from traditional grotesque models, purpose-built for UI and text use.
summary: A grotesque sans with condensed proportions and strategic ink traps, designed specifically for UI interfaces and running text.
description: Designed by Eben Sorkin and Mirko Velimirović for the Spline Team, this grotesque trades traditional proportions for tighter spacing suited to checkout flows, apps and body copy. Ink traps placed at stroke junctions remain subtle at small sizes but open visibly at display scales. Available in five static weights from Light to Bold plus a variable font, with coverage for English and Western European Latin.
foundry_blurb: Spline Sans is described as a grotesque purpose-built for UI interfaces, checkout processes and paragraphs of text. Space efficiency is achieved by condensing traditional grotesque proportions, while strategic thorn traps accent the restrained tone, becoming visible at larger sizes. It supports Google Core through Pro glyph sets for English and Western European languages, and is released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Bebas Neue
classification_notes: An all-caps display face with condensed gothic proportions, used predominantly for headlines and titling rather than body text. Fontshare categorises it as Sans but its narrow, upright letterforms place it firmly in display gothics.
summary: An all-caps gothic display face by Ryoichi Tsunekawa of Dharma Type, built for headlines and titling at large sizes.
description: Originally released through Dharma Type, Bebas Neue became one of the most widely used free display faces on the web, recognised by its tall, narrow, all-caps letterforms drawn from gothic sans traditions. It ships as a single Regular style with 508 glyphs and is distributed here under the SIL Open Font License. Best suited to packaging, editorial headers and screen titles where its blunt uniformity reads clearly from a distance.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Bebas Neue as a display font for headlines, captions and titling, designed by Ryoichi Tsunekawa of Dharma Type and released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Epilogue
classification_notes: Categorised as Sans by Fontshare; the design is a clean, upright grotesque sans with a broad weight range and variable font support, without strong humanist or geometric leanings.
summary: A clean grotesque sans by Tyler Finck of Etcetera Type Co, released as open source with nine weights, matching italics and a variable font axis.
description: Spanning nine weights from Thin to Black with corresponding italics, the family is straightforward and adaptable, suited to headlines, logos and web use. Variable font versions cover both upright and italic styles on a single weight axis, giving useful flexibility for screen environments. Latin script coverage is broad, and the face is distributed under the SIL Open Font License.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare describes Epilogue as a sans serif family in 18 styles, led by Tyler Finck of Etcetera Type Co in Ithaca, New York. It includes a variable style with a weight axis and supports a wide range of languages within the Latin script scope, released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Montserrat
designer: Julieta Ulanovsky, Sol Matas, Juan Pablo del Peral, Jacques Le Bailly
classification_notes: Montserrat draws from the geometric poster lettering of the Buenos Aires neighbourhood of the same name, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with clean, near-circular forms and even stroke widths.
summary: A geometric sans with roots in the poster lettering of the Montserrat neighbourhood of Buenos Aires, available in nine weights with italics and variable font support.
description: Begun by Julieta Ulanovsky in 2010 as a post-graduate project at the University of Buenos Aires, Montserrat takes its forms from the painted signage and architectural lettering of its namesake neighbourhood. The family has grown through successive collaborations, gaining a full weight range, italics, Cyrillic coverage and variable font axes. Its clean geometry and broad weight range make it a go-to for web, packaging and editorial work, though its ubiquity on Google Fonts limits its distinctiveness.
foundry_blurb: Fontshare presents Montserrat as an open-source geometric sans designed by Julieta Ulanovsky and collaborators, offering 18 static styles, two variable fonts, and 1,961 glyphs with Cyrillic support, released under the SIL Open Font License.

name: Be Vietnam Pro
designer: Lam Bao, Tony Le, Vietanh Nguyen
classification_notes: A clean, upright sans-serif designed with particular attention to Vietnamese diacritics and letterforms, placing it within the humanist tradition through its focus on legibility and language-specific refinement.
summary: An open-source sans-serif built around Vietnamese diacritics, offering 18 static styles and a variable font axis for broad digital use.
description: Developed as a refined successor to Be Vietnam, this family was engineered specifically to handle the complex diacritic system of Vietnamese script with adaptive letterforms suited to specific use cases. Eighteen static styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, accompanied by variable font versions for weight-axis flexibility. Broad weight coverage and strong legibility at small sizes make it practical for apps, websites and banner work.
foundry_blurb: Be Vietnam Pro was created to push beyond the original Be Vietnam by refining Vietnamese letterforms with diacritically adaptive forms for specific use cases, improving readability, and enhancing overall functionality. The designers describe it as inheriting the spirit of the original while taking it to the next level, and it is released under the SIL Open Font License.
